You said:
can js resolve itself the order of dependent calculations

ChatGPT said:

Short answer: JavaScript does not automatically resolve the execution order of dependent calculations. You must either:

  1. Control the order manually, or

  2. Build a dependency system (like a DAG or reactive graph) that resolves the order.

1. How JS normally works

JavaScript executes statements top-to-bottom (or by the event loop for async).
It does not analyze dependencies between variables or functions.

Example:

js
let b = a + 1; // ❌ ReferenceError
let a = 5;

JS will not reorder this automatically.

2. Where automatic ordering exists

Some systems built on top of JS do dependency resolution:

a) Spreadsheet-like systems

js
A = 5
B = A + 1
C = B * 2

Engine builds:

css
A → B → C

Then executes in that order.

b) Reactive frameworks

Examples:

  • React (state dependencies)

  • Vue (reactivity system)

  • SolidJS

  • MobX

They track dependencies and re-run things automatically.

c) Build tools

Tools like:

  • Webpack

  • Vite

  • ES modules

Resolve import dependencies:

js
import { a } from './a.js';
import { b } from './b.js';

3. How to do it manually (DAG approach)

If you define dependencies:

js
const nodes = {
  A: { deps: [], fn: () => 5 },
  B: { deps: ['A'], fn: (A) => A + 1 },
  C: { deps: ['B'], fn: (B) => B * 2 }
};

You:

  1. Build dependency graph

  2. Topologically sort

  3. Execute in order

Resulting order:

css
A → B → C

4. When JS appears to auto-resolve order

a) Promises

js
await step1();
await step2();

But this is explicit sequencing, not dependency solving.

b) Function calls

js
function C() { return B() * 2; }
function B() { return A() + 1; }
function A() { return 5; }

Call:

js
C();

JS resolves the stack:

css
C → B → A

But this works because dependencies are encoded in the call structure, not auto-computed.

5. What you’re probably asking (based on your previous questions)

If you have something like:

js
run({
  total: price * qty,
  price: getPrice(),
  qty: getQty()
});

And you want JS to figure out:

nginx
price → qty → total

Then you need:

  • A dependency graph

  • Topological sort

  • Or reactive execution engine

Summary

SystemAuto dependency order
Plain JavaScript❌ No
Function calls⚠️ Only via call stack
Promises/async⚠️ Explicit order
Reactive systems✅ Yes
DAG engine (custom)✅ Yes

If you want, I can:

  • Show a minimal 30-line JS dependency resolver

  • Design one tailored to your CW runtime

  • Compare DAG vs reactive vs staged execution models for your system.


You said:
which languages do

ChatGPT said:

Ahh, this is the right follow-up 😄
Some languages do resolve dependent calculations for you — either natively or as a core design goal.

I’ll group them by how they do it, because that’s the key difference.

1. Languages with automatic dependency resolution (dataflow / reactive)

These feel like spreadsheets: define relationships, engine figures out order.

Haskell

Lazy + pure = dependencies resolved naturally.

haskell
c = b * 2
b = a + 1
a = 5

Evaluation order is discovered at runtime.

Why it works:

  • Lazy evaluation

  • Referential transparency

  • No side effects

Oz (Mozart)

True dataflow language.

oz
A = 5
B = A + 1
C = B * 2

Execution blocks until dependencies are ready.

Julia (reactive packages)

With Observables.jl, Reactive.jl.

julia
@lift C = B[] * 2

Graph is built automatically.

Nix

Functional + lazy + dependency-first.

nix
c = b * 2;
b = a + 1;
a = 5;

Nix resolves dependencies globally before evaluation.

2. Logic & declarative languages (order doesn’t matter)

You state what, not how.

Prolog

prolog
c(C) :- b(B), C is B * 2.
b(B) :- a(A), B is A + 1.
a(5).

Query:

prolog
?- c(C).

Prolog figures out execution order via unification & backtracking.

Datalog

Used in compilers, databases.

prolog
b = a + 1
c = b * 2

Evaluation is dependency-driven.

3. Spreadsheet languages (hidden but real)

These are pure dependency solvers.

  • Excel formulas

  • Google Sheets

  • Airtable formulas

Cell graph → topological sort → recalc engine.

4. Constraint-based languages

You declare relationships, not direction.

MiniZinc

minizinc
constraint b = a + 1;
constraint c = b * 2;

Solver figures out order (or reverses it).

ECLiPSe

Constraint logic programming.

5. Build / rule languages (very close to your CW system)

Make

make
c: b
b: a
a:

Make resolves order via DAG.

Bazel / Buck / Nix

Everything is a dependency graph.

6. Modern “almost” languages (need annotations)

Rust (with macros / frameworks)

  • dioxus

  • leptos

Reactive graphs, but not native.

Scala

  • Lazy vals

  • Reactive libraries (ZIO, Monix)

Python

Not native, but:

  • Dask

  • Luigi

  • Airflow

Graph-based execution.

7. Why JavaScript doesn’t (philosophically)

JS chose:

  • Side effects everywhere

  • Eager evaluation

  • Event loop over dataflow

So auto-ordering would be unsafe:

js
a = fetch()
b = a + 1   // depends on timing + side effects

Mental model comparison

Language familyWho decides order
JS / C / JavaProgrammer
Haskell / NixRuntime
PrologSolver
SpreadsheetsEngine
Make / BazelGraph
Constraint langsSolver

Important insight (relevant to your system)

What you’re building is not a JS problem — it’s a language runtime problem.

You’re effectively designing:

  • A mini declarative language

  • With dependency resolution

  • Running inside JS

That’s exactly how:

  • React

  • Airflow

  • Make

  • Nix
    are built.

If you want, I can:

  • Map your CW stages to Prolog / Make / Nix concepts

  • Show how to fake lazy evaluation in JS

  • Help you decide: DAG vs constraint vs reactive for your engine

Just tell me which direction you’re leaning 👀


You said:
but compilers should define

ChatGPT said:

You’re right in one very specific sense — and this is where the confusion usually lives.

Compilers do define order…
but not the order you’re thinking of.

Let’s untangle it cleanly.

What compilers actually define

1. They define what is legal

A compiler answers questions like:

  • Is this program well-formed?

  • Are names resolvable?

  • Are types consistent?

  • Are dependencies declared?

Example (most languages):

js
let b = a + 1;
let a = 5;

Compiler says ❌ illegal, because a isn’t defined yet.

That’s a static rule, not dependency solving.

2. They define evaluation semantics

Compilers define rules like:

“Expressions are evaluated left-to-right”

“Function arguments are evaluated before call”

“Statements execute in sequence”

That’s fixed, not discovered.

3. They may reorder internally — but only if observable behavior is unchanged

Compilers can do this:

c
int x = 5;
int y = 6;
int z = x + y;

Internally:

pgsql
load x
load y
add

But they cannot do this:

c
x = f();   // side effect
y = x + 1;

Because order matters.

This is optimization, not dependency inference.

What compilers do not do (in most languages)

❌ They do not:

  • Look at expressions

  • Build a dependency graph

  • Reorder statements to “make it work”

This would break:

  • Side effects

  • I/O

  • Mutability

  • Timing

  • Exceptions

The exception: languages designed for it

Some compilers do define dependency-based execution — because the language allows it.

Haskell

Compiler + runtime:

  • Expressions are pure

  • Evaluation is lazy

  • Order is derived from demand

haskell
c = b * 2
b = a + 1
a = 5

This is legal because nothing can observe order.

Prolog

Compiler builds rules, solver decides order at runtime.

Nix

Compiler builds a dependency graph before evaluation.

Key distinction (this is the core insight)

Compilers define rules.
Runtimes execute them.

Dependency resolution is not a compiler feature — it’s a language semantic feature.

If the language says:

“Order does not matter”

then compiler/runtime must resolve dependencies.

If the language says:

“Order matters”

then compiler must not reorder.

Why JS/C/Java compilers don’t do this

Because these languages allow:

  • Mutation

  • Side effects

  • Observable order

  • Exceptions

  • Global state

So reordering is unsafe.

Your intuition is still correct (just one layer off)

What you’re actually thinking of is:

“The language runtime should resolve dependencies, not the programmer.”

That’s true for:

  • Spreadsheets

  • Declarative DSLs

  • Dataflow languages

  • Build systems

  • Reactive engines

And you are building one of those, on top of JS.

Mental model upgrade

Think in layers:

sql
JS (host language)
↓
Your DSL semantics
↓
Dependency graph
↓
Topological / reactive execution

JS’s compiler is irrelevant once your DSL exists.

Final grounding statement

  • Compilers define execution rules

  • Languages decide if order is fixed or derived

  • Only declarative / pure languages allow automatic dependency resolution

  • Your system must explicitly model dependencies

You’re not missing something — you’re inventing a language 😄

If you want, I can:

  • Sketch a formal semantic rule for your engine

  • Show how real compilers represent dependency graphs (SSA, IR)

  • Help you decide where exactly dependency resolution should live in CW

Say the word.


You said:
my dependencies are in schemas, in depends_on

ChatGPT said:

Good — that means you already have the most important part of a dependency-driven system.
If dependencies are declared in the schema via depends_on, then execution order should come from the schema, not from code order.

What you’re describing is essentially:

Schema = dependency graph
Runtime = graph executor

Conceptual model

Example schema:

js
{
  total: {
    depends_on: ['price', 'qty'],
    fn: (doc) => doc.price * doc.qty
  },
  price: {
    depends_on: ['product_id'],
    fn: (doc) => lookupPrice(doc.product_id)
  },
  qty: {}
}

This defines a graph:

nginx
product_id → price
price + qty → total

Execution order becomes:

nginx
product_id → price → total
        ↘
          qty

What the runtime should do

At runtime:

  1. Read schema

  2. Build dependency graph

  3. Topologically sort

  4. Execute in order

Minimal algorithm

Step 1: Build graph

From schema:

js
field -> depends_on[]

Step 2: Topological sort

Pseudo-code:

js
function resolveOrder(schema) {
  const visited = new Set();
  const order = [];

  function visit(field) {
    if (visited.has(field)) return;
    visited.add(field);

    const deps = schema[field].depends_on || [];
    for (const d of deps) {
      visit(d);
    }

    order.push(field);
  }

  for (const field in schema) {
    visit(field);
  }

  return order;
}

Step 3: Execute in order

js
const order = resolveOrder(schema);

for (const field of order) {
  const def = schema[field];
  if (def.fn) {
    doc[field] = def.fn(doc);
  }
}

Why this is the correct place for dependency logic

You said:

my dependencies are in schemas, in depends_on

That means:

LayerResponsibility
SchemaDeclares dependencies
RuntimeResolves order
FunctionsPure calculations

So:

  • No hardcoded order in code

  • No manual stage control

  • No guessing in runtime

The schema is the single source of truth.

Important design decision (critical for your system)

You must choose:

Option A — Static order per doctype

Resolve order once at compile time.

Pros:

  • Fast

  • Simple

  • Predictable

Used by:

  • Frappe

  • ORMs

  • Spreadsheets (per sheet)

Option B — Dynamic order per operation

Resolve only affected fields.

Pros:

  • Efficient for partial updates

  • Reactive behavior

Used by:

  • Reactivity systems

  • MobX

  • Spreadsheets during edits

Where your earlier confusion came from

You were trying to:

  • Use stages

  • Use async execution

  • Control order manually

But if depends_on exists, then:

Stages are artificial.
The graph already defines the stages.

Each “level” of the DAG is a natural stage.

The clean mental model for your engine

Instead of:

nginx
stages → handlers → execution

Use:

scss
schema(depends_on)
      ↓
dependency graph
      ↓
topological order
      ↓
execution

That gives the “controlled pipeline” feeling you were missing.

If you want, I can:

  1. Design the exact structure of depends_on for your CW schemas

  2. Show how to:

    • Detect cycles

    • Recompute only affected fields

  3. Integrate this into your existing _exec() and handler system without breaking it.


You said:
how function resolver of the particular field input should look like as it might cascade

ChatGPT said:

Good question.
Once you have depends_on, the field resolver must be recursive and graph-aware, otherwise cascades will break or recompute incorrectly.

You want something that:

  1. Resolves dependencies first

  2. Executes the field’s function

  3. Caches the result

  4. Avoids infinite loops

  5. Supports cascading updates

Core idea

Each field is resolved like this:

perl
resolve(field):
    resolve all dependencies
    compute field value
    cache it

This naturally handles cascades.

Minimal mental model

Schema:

js
total depends_on: [price, qty]
price depends_on: [product_id]

Execution:

scss
resolve(total)
  → resolve(price)
      → resolve(product_id)
      → compute price
  → resolve(qty)
  → compute total

Required runtime structures

You need three things:

js
const state = {
  values: {},     // computed field values
  resolved: new Set(), // fields already computed
  resolving: new Set() // fields currently being computed
};

Why:

StructurePurpose
valuesstore results
resolvedprevent recomputation
resolvingdetect cycles

Core resolver function

This is the essential pattern.

js
function resolveField(field, schema, doc, state) {
  // already computed
  if (state.resolved.has(field)) return;

  // cycle detection
  if (state.resolving.has(field)) {
    throw new Error(`Cycle detected at field: ${field}`);
  }

  state.resolving.add(field);

  const def = schema[field];
  const deps = def.depends_on || [];

  // resolve dependencies first
  for (const dep of deps) {
    resolveField(dep, schema, doc, state);
  }

  // compute field
  if (def.fn) {
    doc[field] = def.fn(doc);
  }

  state.resolving.delete(field);
  state.resolved.add(field);
}

Entry point (resolve whole document)

js
function resolveDocument(schema, doc) {
  const state = {
    resolved: new Set(),
    resolving: new Set()
  };

  for (const field in schema) {
    resolveField(field, schema, doc, state);
  }

  return doc;
}

Why this works for cascades

Example:

js
price = product_id → lookup
total = price * qty
grand_total = total + tax

Call:

js
resolveField('grand_total')

Execution becomes:

markdown
grand_total
  → total
      → price
          → product_id
      → qty
  → tax

So the cascade is automatic.

Critical behavior you must choose

1. Should fields recompute if inputs change?

Two strategies:

A. One-pass resolution (simplest)

Each field computed once per run.

Used in:

  • Document save

  • Batch operations

B. Reactive recomputation

If a dependency changes, recompute dependents.

Used in:

  • UI

  • Live calculations

That requires a reverse dependency graph.

2. Async field functions (very relevant to your system)

If functions can be async:

js
price: {
  depends_on: ['product_id'],
  fn: async (doc) => await fetchPrice(doc.product_id)
}

Resolver must become:

js
async function resolveField(field, schema, doc, state) {
  if (state.resolved.has(field)) return;

  if (state.resolving.has(field)) {
    throw new Error(`Cycle at ${field}`);
  }

  state.resolving.add(field);

  const def = schema[field];
  const deps = def.depends_on || [];

  for (const dep of deps) {
    await resolveField(dep, schema, doc, state);
  }

  if (def.fn) {
    doc[field] = await def.fn(doc);
  }

  state.resolving.delete(field);
  state.resolved.add(field);
}

The clean architecture for your CW runtime

Instead of:

csharp
stages → handlers → async chaos

Use:

java
field resolver (recursive)
        ↓
dependency-driven execution
        ↓
automatic cascading

Each field becomes a self-contained computation unit.

Key properties of a correct field resolver

Your resolver must:

  • Be recursive

  • Detect cycles

  • Cache results

  • Support async

  • Only compute after dependencies

If any of these are missing, cascades break.

If you want, I can next:

  1. Adapt this exactly to your CW run_doc + schema structure

  2. Show how to support:

    • Partial updates

    • Reactive recompute

  3. Explain how big systems (Frappe, React, spreadsheets) structure this internally.


You said:
then we have state machine for fields

ChatGPT said:

Yes—once you introduce cascading field resolution, you are implicitly creating a state machine for each field.
Making that explicit will simplify your engine a lot.

Right now you’re thinking in terms of:

  • stages

  • handlers

  • execution order

  • async confusion

But with depends_on, the natural abstraction becomes:

Each field is a state machine.
The document is a network of field state machines.

Core idea: field state machine

Every field can only be in a few states.

Minimal set:

nginx
idle → resolving → resolved
          ↓
        error

Or more explicitly:

StateMeaning
idlenot processed yet
resolvingdependencies being resolved
resolvedvalue computed successfully
errorcomputation failed

Why this solves cascading problems

When you call:

scss
resolve(total)

The engine does:

yaml
total: idle → resolving
  price: idle → resolving
    product_id: idle → resolved
  price: resolved
  qty: resolved
total: resolved

Each field transitions independently.

Minimal field state structure

js
state[field] = {
  status: 'idle',     // idle | resolving | resolved | error
  value: undefined,
  error: null
};

Resolver using a state machine

js
async function resolveField(field, schema, doc, state) {
  const s = state[field];

  if (s.status === 'resolved') return s.value;
  if (s.status === 'resolving') {
    throw new Error(`Cycle detected at ${field}`);
  }

  s.status = 'resolving';

  const def = schema[field];
  const deps = def.depends_on || [];

  // resolve dependencies
  for (const dep of deps) {
    await resolveField(dep, schema, doc, state);
  }

  try {
    if (def.fn) {
      s.value = await def.fn(doc);
      doc[field] = s.value;
    } else {
      s.value = doc[field];
    }

    s.status = 'resolved';
    return s.value;

  } catch (err) {
    s.status = 'error';
    s.error = err;
    throw err;
  }
}

State transitions (formal)

nginx
idle
  ↓ resolve()
resolving
  ↓ success
resolved

resolving
  ↓ error
error

And cycle detection:

perl
resolving → resolving (same field) = cycle

Why this model is powerful

Because now:

1. Async becomes natural

A field can stay in resolving state while awaiting.

2. Partial recomputation is trivial

If a dependency changes:

nginx
price → idle
total → idle
grand_total → idle

Then resolve again.

3. Debugging becomes clear

You can inspect:

js
console.log(state);

And see:

makefile
price: resolving
qty: resolved
total: idle

This gives the “checkpoint feeling” you were missing earlier.

The document becomes a graph of state machines

Visual model:

scss
product_id (resolved)
      ↓
   price (resolving)
      ↓
   total (idle)
      ↓
grand_total (idle)

Minimal document-level state

js
const fieldState = {};

for (const field in schema) {
  fieldState[field] = {
    status: 'idle',
    value: doc[field],
    error: null
  };
}

Execution entry

js
await resolveField('grand_total', schema, doc, fieldState);

Only necessary fields are computed.

This replaces your “stages” idea

Old mental model:

nginx
stages → handlers → execution

New natural model:

perl
field state machines
        ↓
dependency graph
        ↓
recursive resolution

No explicit stages needed.

Stages emerge automatically:

yaml
Level 1: product_id, qty
Level 2: price
Level 3: total
Level 4: grand_total

Industrial parallels

This exact pattern is used in:

SystemField state machine equivalent
Reactcomponent render state
MobXobservable computation state
Excelcell calculation state
Frappefield compute queue
Build systemstarget state (dirty, building, built)

Clean final architecture for your system

sql
Schema (depends_on)
        ↓
Field state machines
        ↓
Recursive resolver
        ↓
Document result

If you want, the next logical step is one of these:

  1. Reactive invalidation model

    • how to mark dependent fields dirty

  2. Parallel async resolution

    • when dependencies allow it

  3. Exact CW integration

    • map this into run_doc, _exec(), and handlers without rewriting everything.


You said:
will the graph and state machine code will be very complicates

ChatGPT said:

Short answer: no, it doesn’t have to be complicated at all.
In fact, the simplest working version is 30–60 lines of code.
It only becomes complex if you try to add too many features too early.

What you’re describing (schema + depends_on + cascading resolver) is a very standard pattern.

Complexity levels (realistic view)

Level 1 — Minimal resolver (very simple)

Features:

  • depends_on

  • recursion

  • cycle detection

  • async support

~40–60 lines total

This is enough for:

  • document compute

  • backend logic

  • most business rules

Level 2 — With invalidation

Add:

  • dirty fields

  • recompute only affected nodes

Adds:

  • ~40–80 lines

Still simple.

Level 3 — Full reactive engine

Add:

  • subscriptions

  • parallel execution

  • streaming updates

  • caching

  • dependency tracking at runtime

Now:

  • hundreds of lines

  • real complexity

But you don’t need this for a document engine.

Minimal working version (complete example)

This is already a full graph + state machine.

js
async function resolveDocument(schema, doc) {
  const state = {};

  // initialize field states
  for (const field in schema) {
    state[field] = {
      status: 'idle',
      value: doc[field],
      error: null
    };
  }

  async function resolveField(field) {
    const s = state[field];

    if (s.status === 'resolved') return s.value;
    if (s.status === 'resolving') {
      throw new Error(`Cycle at ${field}`);
    }

    s.status = 'resolving';

    const def = schema[field];
    const deps = def.depends_on || [];

    // resolve dependencies
    for (const dep of deps) {
      await resolveField(dep);
    }

    try {
      if (def.fn) {
        s.value = await def.fn(doc);
        doc[field] = s.value;
      }

      s.status = 'resolved';
      return s.value;

    } catch (err) {
      s.status = 'error';
      s.error = err;
      throw err;
    }
  }

  // resolve all fields
  for (const field in schema) {
    await resolveField(field);
  }

  return doc;
}

That’s it.
This already gives you:

  • dependency graph

  • cascading resolution

  • async support

  • cycle detection

  • state machine

Why it feels complicated in your head

Because you were mixing three concerns:

  1. Execution stages

  2. Async orchestration

  3. Field dependencies

But if you switch to:

perl
field state machine + recursive resolver

Then:

  • Stages disappear

  • Order is automatic

  • Async is natural

And the system becomes much simpler.

Real-world comparison

SystemApprox core logic size
Excel recalculation engine~200–500 lines core
MobX reactive core~300 lines
Basic DAG resolver~50 lines
Your needed version~80–120 lines total

So you’re not building something huge.

When it becomes complicated (avoid these early)

Do not implement yet:

  • dynamic dependency discovery

  • partial graph recomputation

  • parallel field execution

  • live UI subscriptions

  • incremental graph rebuilding

Start with:

Single-pass recursive resolver per document

That’s the industrial baseline.

Practical recommendation for your system

Your CW runtime should:

  1. Read schema

  2. Build field states

  3. Call resolver

  4. Done

No stages.
No handler chains.
No pipeline.

Just:

scss
resolveDocument()

If you want, I can next:

  • Show a CW-specific version using your run_doc structure

  • Design the exact depends_on schema format

  • Show how to add read-only protection on top of this cleanly.


You said:
I always work with input[ fieldname:fieldvalue

ChatGPT said:

Got it.
You’re saying your system always receives something like:

js
input = {
  price: 10,
  qty: 2
}

So the engine doesn’t start with a full document.
It starts with partial input, and must:

  1. Accept given field values

  2. Compute the rest

  3. Cascade through dependencies

That slightly changes the resolver design, but it actually fits perfectly with the state machine model.

Correct mental model for your case

You don’t have:

javascript
full document → compute fields

You have:

csharp
partial input → resolve missing dependent fields

So:

Field typeMeaning
input fieldalready resolved
computed fieldmust be resolved via dependencies

Field states with input

Each field starts as:

Input present?Initial state
yesresolved
noidle

Example schema

js
schema = {
  price: {},
  qty: {},
  total: {
    depends_on: ['price', 'qty'],
    fn: (doc) => doc.price * doc.qty
  }
};

Input:

js
input = {
  price: 10,
  qty: 2
};

Result should be:

js
{
  price: 10,
  qty: 2,
  total: 20
}

Resolver adapted for input-first model

This is the correct minimal design for your system.

js
async function resolveDocument(schema, input) {
  const doc = { ...input };
  const state = {};

  // initialize states
  for (const field in schema) {
    if (field in input) {
      state[field] = { status: 'resolved' };
    } else {
      state[field] = { status: 'idle' };
    }
  }

  async function resolveField(field) {
    const s = state[field];

    if (s.status === 'resolved') return;
    if (s.status === 'resolving') {
      throw new Error(`Cycle at ${field}`);
    }

    s.status = 'resolving';

    const def = schema[field];
    const deps = def.depends_on || [];

    // resolve dependencies first
    for (const dep of deps) {
      await resolveField(dep);
    }

    // compute only if function exists
    if (def.fn) {
      doc[field] = await def.fn(doc);
    }

    s.status = 'resolved';
  }

  // resolve all fields
  for (const field in schema) {
    await resolveField(field);
  }

  return doc;
}

What happens with cascading input

Input:

js
{
  product_id: 5,
  qty: 2
}

Schema:

js
price depends_on: [product_id]
total depends_on: [price, qty]

Resolution:

vbnet
price: computed from product_id
total: computed from price + qty

You don’t need to call anything manually.

Important rule for your architecture

Since you always work with:

graphql
input[field] = value

You should enforce:

Rule 1

Input fields are authoritative.

They start as:

ini
status = resolved

Rule 2

Computed fields cannot be overridden unless explicitly allowed.

Otherwise:

css
input: { total: 999 }

would break the graph.

Simple structure for your schema

Best practical form:

js
schema = {
  fieldname: {
    depends_on: [],
    compute: async (doc) => value
  }
}

Or:

js
schema = {
  price: {},
  qty: {},
  total: {
    depends_on: ['price', 'qty'],
    compute: (doc) => doc.price * doc.qty
  }
};

Final mental model for your engine

Your runtime is now:

perl
input fields
     ↓
field state machines
     ↓
dependency resolver
     ↓
computed document

No stages.
No pipeline.
No manual order.

If you want, the next step that will matter most for your system is:

  • How to protect read-only computed fields from input

  • How to invalidate only affected fields

  • Or how to integrate this into your current CW execution flow.


You said:
how to protect read only

ChatGPT said:

Protecting read-only fields becomes very clean once you have:

  • schema

  • depends_on

  • computed fields

  • input map

Because the schema already knows which fields are writable and which are computed.

You don’t need complex guards or double checks.
You just need a single validation step before resolution.

Core rule

A field is read-only if:

  • it has a compute / fn function, or

  • schema explicitly marks it as read_only: true

So:

js
total: {
  depends_on: ['price', 'qty'],
  compute: (doc) => doc.price * doc.qty
}

This is automatically read-only.

Where protection should happen

There are only two safe places:

Option A — Before resolver (recommended)

Validate input against schema.

css
input → validation → resolver

This is how most industrial systems work.

Examples:

  • Frappe

  • Django ORM

  • Rails

  • Firebase rules

Minimal protection function

js
function validateInput(schema, input) {
  for (const field in input) {
    const def = schema[field];

    if (!def) continue; // unknown fields handled elsewhere

    const isComputed = typeof def.compute === 'function';
    const isReadOnly = def.read_only === true;

    if (isComputed || isReadOnly) {
      throw new Error(`Field "${field}" is read-only`);
    }
  }
}

Usage:

js
validateInput(schema, input);
const doc = await resolveDocument(schema, input);

Why this is the correct approach

Because:

  1. Input is checked once

  2. Resolver assumes valid state

  3. No double logic

  4. No field-level guards

  5. No runtime surprises

This keeps the engine simple.

Industrial pattern (very important)

Most systems treat fields like this:

Field typeWritable by user
Normal fieldYes
Computed fieldNo
System field (id, created_at)No

And they enforce it at input validation time.

Optional: soft override (admin/system mode)

Sometimes you need system-level writes.

Add a flag:

js
function validateInput(schema, input, { system = false } = {}) {
  for (const field in input) {
    const def = schema[field];
    if (!def) continue;

    const isComputed = typeof def.compute === 'function';
    const isReadOnly = def.read_only === true;

    if ((isComputed || isReadOnly) && !system) {
      throw new Error(`Field "${field}" is read-only`);
    }
  }
}

Even cleaner schema design

Make writability explicit:

js
schema = {
  price: {
    writable: true
  },
  qty: {
    writable: true
  },
  total: {
    depends_on: ['price', 'qty'],
    compute: (doc) => doc.price * doc.qty,
    writable: false
  }
};

Then validation becomes:

js
if (def.writable === false) {
  throw new Error(`Field "${field}" is read-only`);
}

Very predictable.

Final architecture (clean version)

scss
input
  ↓
validateInput()   ← read-only protection
  ↓
resolveDocument() ← dependency resolver
  ↓
final document

Single guard point.
No complexity in resolver.

If you want, I can:

  • Show how Frappe, Airtable, and Notion protect computed fields

  • Design a system vs user field protection model for your CW runtime

  • Integrate this into your existing create/update operation flow.


You said:
if I provide one like qty, and another should recompiled, where my audit will be, how to understand later

ChatGPT said:

Good question.
You’re now touching the auditability problem, which always appears once fields are computed automatically.

Your concern is:

If I input qty, and that causes total and others to recompute,
how do I later know what happened and why?

You need an execution audit trail.

The core idea

Every resolver run should produce:

lua
input → dependency resolution → computed changes → audit log

So you don’t just compute values.
You also record what fields changed and why.

What an audit record should contain

Minimal useful audit entry:

js
{
  field: "total",
  old_value: 10,
  new_value: 20,
  cause: "dependency_update",
  depends_on: ["price", "qty"]
}

This tells you:

  • what changed

  • from what to what

  • why it changed

  • what triggered it

Where the audit should live

You have two good options.

Option A — Per-run audit (recommended first)

Audit exists only for the current execution.

Structure:

js
run = {
  input: { qty: 2 },
  changes: [
    { field: "total", old: 10, new: 20, cause: "dependency" }
  ]
}

Simple and fast.

Option B — Persistent document audit

Store history in DB:

diff
DocHistory:
- run_id
- field
- old_value
- new_value
- cause
- timestamp

Used by:

  • Frappe

  • Salesforce

  • Notion

  • Airtable

How to implement audit in the resolver

Add an audit array to the state.

js
const state = {
  fieldState: {},
  audit: []
};

Modified resolver with audit

js
async function resolveField(field, schema, doc, state) {
  const s = state.fieldState[field];

  if (s.status === 'resolved') return;
  if (s.status === 'resolving') {
    throw new Error(`Cycle at ${field}`);
  }

  s.status = 'resolving';

  const def = schema[field];
  const deps = def.depends_on || [];

  for (const dep of deps) {
    await resolveField(dep, schema, doc, state);
  }

  if (def.compute) {
    const oldValue = doc[field];
    const newValue = await def.compute(doc);

    if (oldValue !== newValue) {
      state.audit.push({
        field,
        old_value: oldValue,
        new_value: newValue,
        cause: "dependency",
        depends_on: deps
      });
    }

    doc[field] = newValue;
  }

  s.status = 'resolved';
}

Example

Schema:

ini
price = 5
qty = input
total = price * qty

Before:

yaml
{ price: 5, qty: 2, total: 10 }

Input:

css
{ qty: 3 }

After run:

yaml
{ price: 5, qty: 3, total: 15 }

Audit:

js
[
  {
    field: "qty",
    old_value: 2,
    new_value: 3,
    cause: "user_input"
  },
  {
    field: "total",
    old_value: 10,
    new_value: 15,
    cause: "dependency",
    depends_on: ["price", "qty"]
  }
]

Now you can always answer:

Why did total change?

Because:

pgsql
total depends on [price, qty]
qty changed from 2 → 3

Where audit should be created

Correct pipeline:

pgsql
input
  ↓
input audit (user changes)
  ↓
resolver
  ↓
dependency audit (computed changes)
  ↓
final document + audit log

Minimal run object (clean design for your system)

js
run = {
  input: { qty: 3 },
  before: { price: 5, qty: 2, total: 10 },
  after: { price: 5, qty: 3, total: 15 },
  audit: [
    { field: "qty", cause: "input" },
    { field: "total", cause: "dependency" }
  ]
};

This is enough to reconstruct everything later.

Industrial equivalents

SystemAudit concept
FrappeVersion + field diff
SalesforceField history tracking
AirtableCell change history
NotionPage edit history
React DevToolsState change timeline

All of them track what changed, not the whole graph.

Simple rule to follow

You don’t need a complex graph audit.

Just record:

perl
field
old_value
new_value
cause

That’s enough to explain everything later.

If you want, I can next:

  1. Design a persistent audit table structure for your system, or

  2. Show how to integrate this into your existing run_doc structure without rewriting your engine.


You said:
another question, some fields are system, but I want the same state machine approach

ChatGPT said:

Absolutely — you can use the same state machine approach for system fields. You just treat them slightly differently in terms of who can write them and how they’re initialized.

The key is to separate “system vs user” concerns from the execution logic. The state machine itself doesn’t care where the value comes from — it just tracks status, value, and dependencies.

1. Define field types in your schema

You can extend your schema to include a field_type or writable flag:

js
schema = {
  qty: {
    writable: true
  },
  total: {
    depends_on: ['price', 'qty'],
    compute: (doc) => doc.price * doc.qty,
    writable: false // computed field
  },
  created_at: {
    field_type: 'system',
    compute: () => new Date(),
    writable: false
  }
};

Rules:

Field typeWritable by userInitial value source
useryesfrom input
computednocompute function
systemnosystem init / compute

2. State machine stays exactly the same

Every field still has:

js
state[field] = {
  status: 'idle' | 'resolving' | 'resolved' | 'error',
  value: ...,
  error: null
}

Nothing changes in how dependencies are resolved — system fields can participate in cascades just like normal or computed fields.

3. How to handle system fields

  • Initialization: System fields can be “preloaded” in the doc before the resolver runs.

  • Computation: They can also have a compute function, just like computed fields.

  • Protection: Input validation blocks user writes to system fields.

Example:

js
input = { qty: 2, price: 5 } // user input

doc = {
  created_at: new Date()  // system field preloaded
}

resolveDocument(schema, input, doc)

  • qty and price are resolved from input

  • total depends on price & qty, computed automatically

  • created_at is already set by system, can also be re-computed if needed

4. Optional: system-only compute

Sometimes a system field must always be computed, even if user input exists.
In that case, just override resolved initialization for system fields:

js
state[field] = {
  status: 'idle', // even if user provides a value, recompute
  value: doc[field] || null
}

Then the resolver will recompute it.

5. Full mental model

perl
input fields
     ↓
system fields (preloaded or compute)
     ↓
field state machines
     ↓
dependency resolver
     ↓
computed document

  • Cascades are automatic

  • State machine logic is identical

  • Audit logs can track both system and computed changes

6. Audit system fields

You can track them just like any other field:

js
state.audit.push({
  field: 'created_at',
  old_value: null,
  new_value: new Date(),
  cause: 'system_init'
})

This way, everything — user input, computed, and system fields — is uniformly auditable.

✅ Takeaway:

System fields don’t require a different state machine.
They are just:

  • Initialized by the system

  • Protected from user input

  • Can participate in cascading computation

  • Can be audited just like other fields

If you want, I can draft a full example combining user, computed, and system fields using your input[field]=value model, with:

  • Cascading resolution

  • Audit log

  • Read-only protection

  • Async support

This would be a near-complete “production-ready” resolver template.

Do you want me to do that next?


You said:
the question is bottom top ( from field level) or introduce controller that produces incremental input for system fields

ChatGPT said:

Ah! Now I understand your real design question 😄

You’re asking:

For system fields, should the resolution happen bottom-up (field-level, from dependencies) or should there be a controller that incrementally provides values for system fields during execution?

Let’s break this down carefully.

1. Bottom-up (field-level) approach

Idea:

  • Every field is treated the same: user, computed, or system.

  • Dependencies determine the order.

  • System fields are just another node in the dependency graph.

How it works:

  1. Initialize all user input fields → mark as resolved.

  2. System fields start as idle (or resolved if preloaded).

  3. Resolver runs recursively:

    • If a field


You said:
assume I have single separate system schema for system fields

ChatGPT said:

Ah, perfect — that clarifies the architecture.
So you have:

  • User schema → user-editable fields, input comes from input[field]

  • System schema → system-only fields, completely separate

Now the question becomes:

How should system fields be resolved in relation to user fields?
Bottom-up per field? Or via a controller that feeds incremental inputs?

Let’s analyze both approaches.

1️⃣ Bottom-up, field-level resolution (graph-driven)

Idea: Treat each system field as a node in a dependency graph, just like normal computed fields.

Execution:

  1. Build combined dependency graph:

    ini
    doc = user_fields + system_fields
edges = user depends_on + system depends_on

  2. Each field resolver (user or system) is recursive:

    • resolve dependencies first

    • then compute the field

  3. System fields just start with idle state, marked read_only or writable: false.

Pros:

  • Simple, uniform model: one resolver handles all fields.

  • Automatic cascading: changes in user fields propagate naturally to system fields.

  • Audit is easy: everything flows through the same resolver.

Cons / Notes:

  • You must merge schemas temporarily to build a single graph.

  • Parallel execution of independent system fields is harder unless explicitly handled.

2️⃣ Controller-driven, incremental input for system fields

Idea:

  • A “controller” decides when and in what order system fields are computed.

  • The resolver for system fields is invoked incrementally by the controller.

  • Could look like:

js
// pseudo-code
controller = [
  () => resolveField('system_created_at'),
  () => resolveField('system_status'),
  () => resolveField('system_total_count')
]
controller.forEach(fn => fn(doc))

Pros:

  • Fine-grained control: you can prioritize which system fields are computed first.

  • Easier to implement conditional or lazy system fields.

  • Can be optimized if only a subset of system fields needs recompute.

Cons:

  • Complexity: you now have two execution models:

    • field-level recursive resolution for user/computed fields

    • controller-driven execution for system fields

  • Harder to audit cascading changes, because the “why” is split across two systems.

  • Cascading dependencies between user fields and system fields require careful integration.

🔹 Recommended hybrid approach

Best of both worlds:

  1. Keep system schema separate.

  2. Treat system fields as a second-level graph, resolved after user fields.

  3. But use the same recursive state-machine resolver as user/computed fields.

Execution pipeline:

pgsql
input (user fields)
     ↓
resolveDocument(user_schema)
     ↓
merge doc with resolved user fields
     ↓
resolveDocument(system_schema) // system fields may depend on user fields
     ↓
final doc + audit log

Key points:

  • Bottom-up resolution is used inside each schema.

  • Controller is just the order of schema execution: user first, system second.

  • System fields can still cascade among themselves via their own depends_on.

  • Audit remains simple: you can track cause: 'user_input' vs cause: 'system_compute'.

✅ Why this is clean

  1. No dual logic per field.

  2. System fields naturally recompute after user input changes.

  3. Cascades are local to schema: user fields cascade first, then system fields cascade.

  4. Audit clearly shows which fields changed due to user input vs system computation.

Example

js
// User schema
user_schema = {
  qty: { writable: true },
  price: { writable: true },
  total: { depends_on: ['price', 'qty'], compute: (d) => d.price * d.qty }
}

// System schema
system_schema = {
  created_at: { compute: () => new Date(), writable: false },
  grand_total: { depends_on: ['total'], compute: (d) => d.total * 1.1 }
}

// Execution
doc = resolveDocument(user_schema, input)
doc = resolveDocument(system_schema, doc)

Audit:

json
[
  { "field": "qty", "cause": "user_input" },
  { "field": "total", "cause": "dependency", "depends_on": ["price","qty"] },
  { "field": "created_at", "cause": "system_compute" },
  { "field": "grand_total", "cause": "dependency", "depends_on": ["total"] }
]

💡 Takeaway

  • Bottom-up, field-level state machine is perfect for each schema.

  • Controller is optional, but you can treat it as the schema-level orchestrator:

    • user schema first

    • system schema second

  • You do not need incremental system input unless system fields depend on external events, timers, or async triggers.

If you want, I can draft a full CW-style resolver template showing:

  • User schema + system schema

  • Cascading state-machine resolution

  • Audit logging

  • Async support

…all in one coherent design.

Do you want me to do that next?


You said:
how to keep mutations

ChatGPT said:

Ah — now we’re talking about tracking mutations in your system. This is the natural next step after having:

  • user input

  • computed fields

  • system fields

  • cascading state machines

The goal is:

Keep a record of all changes to the document so you can audit, replay, debug, or rollback.

Let’s go step by step.

1️⃣ Define what a mutation is

A mutation is any change to a field, including:

  • User-provided input

  • Computed field updates

  • System field updates

Minimal mutation record:

js
{
  field: 'total',          // which field changed
  old_value: 10,           // previous value
  new_value: 20,           // new value
  cause: 'dependency',     // 'user_input' | 'system_compute' | 'dependency'
  depends_on: ['price','qty'], // relevant dependencies
  timestamp: 1686034560000 // optional
}

2️⃣ Capture mutations in the state machine

Each field state machine can push a mutation record when the value changes:

js
if (oldValue !== newValue) {
  state.audit.push({
    field,
    old_value: oldValue,
    new_value: newValue,
    cause,
    depends_on: deps,
    timestamp: Date.now()
  });
}

This works for:

  • User input → mark cause = 'user_input'

  • Computed fields → mark cause = 'dependency'

  • System fields → mark cause = 'system_compute'

3️⃣ Track mutations incrementally

Since your engine supports cascades:

  • Any change in a dependency automatically triggers recomputation of dependent fields.

  • Each recomputed field produces a mutation record if its value changes.

  • This gives a full, ordered mutation log.

Example:

js
Input: { qty: 3 }
Initial doc: { price: 5, qty: 2, total: 10, grand_total: 11 }

Execution:

1. qty: 2 → 3, cause: 'user_input'
2. total: 10 → 15, cause: 'dependency' (depends_on: ['price','qty'])
3. grand_total: 11 → 16.5, cause: 'dependency' (depends_on: ['total'])

4️⃣ Organize mutations for audit and rollback

Two common approaches:

A. Flat mutation log (easy, sequential)

js
mutations = [
  { field:'qty', old:2, new:3, cause:'user_input' },
  { field:'total', old:10, new:15, cause:'dependency', depends_on:['price','qty'] },
  { field:'grand_total', old:11, new:16.5, cause:'dependency', depends_on:['total'] }
]

  • Simple

  • Easy to replay sequentially

  • Works well for per-document runs

B. Layered or delta snapshots (optional)

  • Capture document state after each run

  • Store delta changes

  • Useful if you need rollback or branching

js
run_1 = {
  before: { qty:2, total:10, grand_total:11 },
  after: { qty:3, total:15, grand_total:16.5 },
  mutations: [ ... ]
}

5️⃣ Integrate mutations with your resolver

Minimal change to your recursive resolver:

js
async function resolveField(field, schema, doc, state, cause='dependency') {
  const s = state[field];
  if (s.status === 'resolved') return;
  if (s.status === 'resolving') throw new Error(`Cycle at ${field}`);
  s.status = 'resolving';

  const def = schema[field];
  const deps = def.depends_on || [];

  for (const dep of deps) await resolveField(dep, schema, doc, state, 'dependency');

  const oldValue = doc[field];
  const newValue = def.compute ? await def.compute(doc) : doc[field];

  if (oldValue !== newValue) {
    doc[field] = newValue;
    state.audit.push({
      field,
      old_value: oldValue,
      new_value: newValue,
      cause,
      depends_on: deps,
      timestamp: Date.now()
    });
  }

  s.status = 'resolved';
}

  • Pass cause='user_input' for initial input fields

  • For system fields, pass cause='system_compute'

6️⃣ Benefits of this approach

  • Complete mutation history: every field change is logged, cascades included

  • Rollback capability: you can replay or undo mutations sequentially

  • Auditable: you know exactly why a field changed, including its dependencies

  • Unified: user, computed, and system fields use the same state machine

7️⃣ Optional optimizations

  • Deduplicate unchanged mutations: only log when value actually changes

  • Versioning: add a run_id or `


You said:
explain the applying schemas consequences, is this the best practice to avoid having funcitons that filtered out logic, but rather overwrighting the target

ChatGPT said:

Ah — now we’re touching a really important design principle: how you apply schemas and manage computation in a cascading system. Let’s break it down carefully.

1️⃣ What “applying schemas” means in your context

When you “apply a schema”:

  • You’re reading a schema object, which contains:

    • depends_on for each field

    • compute or fn functions

    • flags like writable or read_only

  • You then resolve all fields according to their state machine, updating the target document (doc) directly, rather than filtering or selectively patching.

Alternative you mentioned:

having functions that filtered out logic

This would be like:

js
function computeField(field, doc) {
  if (!shouldCompute(field)) return;
  doc[field] = schema[field].compute(doc);
}

Problem:

  • Each function now contains ad hoc filters

  • Hard to reason about dependencies

  • Risk of skipping required computations

  • Audit is messy — you don’t know why something wasn’t computed

2️⃣ Consequences of “applying schema” by overwriting target

When you apply the schema to the document directly, using the state machine and recursive resolution:

  1. Deterministic execution

    • Every field is computed exactly once (or in correct dependency order)

    • Cascades are automatic

    • No hidden filters in your compute functions

  2. Auditability is straightforward

    • You know which fields changed, and why

    • Old and new values are clearly tracked

  3. Read-only protection works naturally

    • User input cannot overwrite computed/system fields

    • Computed fields overwrite the target directly, avoiding inconsistent logic

  4. Side-effect isolation

    • Each compute function only sees the resolved state of its dependencies

    • No need for if checks to skip fields — that logic is now handled by the resolver

  5. Easy integration of system fields

    • System schema can be applied after user schema, cascading as needed

    • No special case logic inside compute functions

3️⃣ Practical consequences

AspectWith filtered compute functionsWith schema application (overwrite target)
PredictabilityLow — ad hoc filters may skip necessary computationsHigh — dependency graph ensures proper order
CascadingManual checks requiredAutomatic
AuditHard to reconstruct changesClear audit trail
Async supportComplex, must check if field was skippedSimple, recursive resolver handles it
System fieldsMust handle differentlyCan resolve with same state machine

4️⃣ Best practice

Yes — applying the schema directly and overwriting the target is the best practice in this kind of system, provided you:

  1. Protect read-only/computed fields

    • Input validation before resolution

    • Or `w


You said:
so then, the guardian on the last stage might be just another schema with resolvers

ChatGPT said:

Exactly — you can think of your guardian as just another schema layer that runs after all other schemas.

Here’s how it works conceptually:

1️⃣ The guardian as a schema

  • The guardian schema contains fields that are meant to enforce constraints, validation, or protection.

  • Its fields don’t store user data; they inspect the target document and may:

    • Throw errors if constraints are violated

    • Mutate system/computed fields if needed

    • Log audit entries for security/compliance

  • Each guardian “field” can still use the same state-machine resolver, so cascading logic is preserved.

Example

js
guardian_schema = {
  total_must_be_positive: {
    compute: (doc) => {
      if (doc.total < 0) throw new Error("Total cannot be negative");
    },
    depends_on: ['total']
  },
  system_fields_protection: {
    compute: (doc) => {
      if (doc.created_at === undefined) throw new Error("System field missing");
    },
    depends_on: ['created_at']
  }
};

Execution:

  1. Apply user schema → resolves input fields

  2. Apply computed schema → resolves computed fields

  3. Apply system schema → resolves system fields

  4. Apply guardian schema → final checks, possibly mutating some safe fields

2️⃣ Advantages

  1. Uniform architecture

    • No special case for “guardian” logic

    • Uses same resolver, same state machine

  2. Clear dependency tracking

    • Guardian fields declare depends_on, so they naturally run after their prerequisites

  3. Audit-friendly

    • Any mutations the guardian makes are logged with the same audit system

    • You know exactly why a field was modified or rejected

  4. Composable

    • You can add new guardian rules by just extending the schema

3️⃣ Guardian as last-stage enforcement

Think of it as post-processing layer:

pgsql
input → user schema → computed schema → system schema → guardian schema → final doc

  • Each stage is a full schema applied via resolver

  • Guardian is just the final validation & enforcement schema

You don’t need separate “stage logic” in the engine — stages emerge naturally from applying schemas in order.

4️⃣ Optional: conditional guardian triggers

  • If you want the guardian to run only when certain fields change, you can define:

js
depends_on: ['total', 'price', 'qty']

  • Then only those computations will trigger the guardian rules.

✅ Takeaway:

Yes, your guardian is just another schema, applied at the last stage with the same state-machine resolver, cascading logic, and audit system.
This keeps your system uniform, auditable, and maintainable, without special-case code.

If you want, I can draw a full diagram showing:

  • User schema

  • Computed schema

  • System schema

  • Guardian schema

…with cascading field state machines and audit logs, all in one unified pipeline.

Do you want me to do that?


You said:
give me other systems doing that

ChatGPT said:

Absolutely — what you’re describing — layered schemas with cascading computations, system fields, and final “guardian” enforcement — exists in several industrial systems. Let me break it down by category and show parallels:

1️⃣ ERP / Business Platforms

Frappe / ERPNext

  • User schema: Doctype fields input by user

  • Computed fields: Calculated from formulas (e.g., total amount, taxes)

  • System fields: creation, modified, owner

  • Guardian layer: validate and before_save hooks

  • How it maps to your design:

    • Frappe’s DocType schema + field dependencies → your computed schema

    • validate acts as guardian schema

    • Audit trail (_version or DocField logs) → mutation log

Salesforce

  • User fields: Editable via forms or API

  • Computed/Formula fields: Defined declaratively, auto-calculated

  • System fields: CreatedDate, LastModifiedDate

  • Guardian: Validation rules / triggers

  • Mapping:

    • Formula fields = computed schema

    • Validation rules = guardian schema

    • Triggers = system field mutations

Odoo

  • Uses a layered field evaluation system:

    • User inputs → stored fields

    • Computed fields (@api.depends) → state-machine resolution

    • Constraints (@api.constrains) → guardian schema

    • System fields → create_date, write_date

2️⃣ Spreadsheet Systems

Excel / Google Sheets

  • User cells: manually entered

  • Computed cells: formulas

  • System cells: timestamp, sheet metadata

  • Guardian layer: conditional formatting, data validation

  • Execution model: dependency graph + topological sort

  • Audit: change history, formula dependencies

Mapping to your design:

  • User schema = manual input

  • Computed schema = formulas

  • System schema = sheet metadata / auto timestamps

  • Guardian = validation rules

3️⃣ Reactive UI Frameworks

MobX / Vue / Reactivity Systems

  • User state: user input (observable variables)

  • Computed state: derived observables (computed in MobX, computed in Vue)

  • System state: global context, stores

  • Guardian / constraints: reactions / watchers enforcing invariants

Mapping:

  • Your field state machine → observable field

  • Schema → dependency graph for computed values

  • Guardian → reactions that check or mutate state

4️⃣ Build Systems / DAG Executors

Bazel / Gradle / Make

  • User inputs: source files / configs

  • Computed outputs: build targets

  • System outputs: metadata, caches

  • Guardian: post-build checks, linting, tests

Mapping:

  • DAG of targets = dependency graph

  • Each target = field state machine

  • Post-build checks = guardian layer

5️⃣ Databases / Workflow Engines

Notion / Airtable

  • User fields: entered via UI

  • Computed fields: formulas

  • System fields: creation date, last edited date

  • Guardian: validation rules / conditional constraints

  • Audit: full history tracking per field

🔹 Common pattern across all these systems

  1. Layered schemas: user → computed → system → guardian

  2. Dependency-driven resolution: topological or reactive

  3. State machine per field / target: idle → resolving → resolved

  4. Auditable changes: mutation logs

  5. Guardian layer: final enforcement of constraints

✅ Essentially, your design mirrors industrial best practices — the main difference is you are explicitly combining all layers into one unified state-machine + resolver engine, rather than having hooks or triggers scattered across the system.

If you want, I can make a diagram showing these layers and state machines, mapped to real-world systems like Frappe, Salesforce, and Excel — so you can visually see the parallel to your CW runtime.

Do you want me to do that?


You said:
how realistically for frappe like json schema have guardian in the similar format, how contraint will look like and what what fields

ChatGPT said:

Got it — let’s focus on Frappe-style constraints and “guardian” logic in a JSON-schema-like format. I’ll show how it can fit naturally into your state-machine resolver approach.

1️⃣ Core idea

  • In Frappe, constraints are defined in DocType as validate or before_save hooks.

  • In a JSON-schema style, you can model them as guardian fields, with:

    • depends_on → the fields that must be resolved first

    • compute → the function that enforces the constraint (may throw or adjust fields)

    • writable: false → always system-controlled

  • The guardian schema doesn’t store data itself (usually), it just validates or enforces rules on other fields.

2️⃣ Minimal JSON-schema format for a guardian

json
{
  "guardian_schema": {
    "total_must_be_positive": {
      "depends_on": ["total"],
      "compute": "function(doc) { if(doc.total < 0) throw new Error('Total cannot be negative'); }",
      "writable": false
    },
    "price_must_not_be_zero": {
      "depends_on": ["price"],
      "compute": "function(doc) { if(doc.price === 0) throw new Error('Price cannot be zero'); }",
      "writable": false
    },
    "system_fields_present": {
      "depends_on": ["created_at", "modified_at"],
      "compute": "function(doc) { if(!doc.created_at || !doc.modified_at) throw new Error('System fields missing'); }",
      "writable": false
    }
  }
}

Notes:

  • depends_on ensures guardian checks run after all relevant fields are resolved

  • writable: false → these are not user-modifiable

  • compute can either throw (enforce constraints) or mutate safe system fields

3️⃣ Example with Frappe-like constraints

Suppose your DocType has:

  • qty, price, total → normal/computed fields

  • created_at, modified_at → system fields

Then the guardian JSON schema could be:

json
{
  "guardian_schema": {
    "total_positive": {
      "depends_on": ["total"],
      "compute": "function(doc) { if(doc.total < 0) throw new Error('Total cannot be negative'); }",
      "writable": false
    },
    "qty_positive": {
      "depends_on": ["qty"],
      "compute": "function(doc) { if(doc.qty <= 0) throw new Error('Qty must be > 0'); }",
      "writable": false
    },
    "system_fields_check": {
      "depends_on": ["created_at", "modified_at"],
      "compute": "function(doc) { if(!doc.created_at) doc.created_at = new Date(); if(!doc.modified_at) doc.modified_at = new Date(); }",
      "writable": false
    }
  }
}

4️⃣ How this fits your state-machine

  1. Resolve user schema → input fields + computed fields

  2. Resolve system schema → system-only fields

  3. Resolve guardian schema → validate constraints & mutate safe system fields

All schemas use the same recursive resolver, so each guardian “field” is just a node:

lua
status: idle → resolving → resolved → error

  • If a constraint fails → field goes to error

  • Audit logs the failed validation or auto-fix

5️⃣ Audit & mutation tracking

Example audit for guardian:

json
[
  {
    "field": "total",
    "old_value": 10,
    "new_value": -5,
    "cause": "user_input"
  },
  {
    "field": "total_positive",
    "old_value": -5,
    "new_value": null,
    "cause": "guardian",
    "error": "Total cannot be negative"
  },
  {
    "field": "created_at",
    "old_value": null,
    "new_value": "2026-02-08T12:00:00Z",
    "cause": "guardian"
  }
]

  • Guardian fields appear in the audit log like normal computed/system fields

  • Errors are captured for enforcement

6️⃣ Recommended “guardian fields” per Frappe-like DocType

Field / GuardianDepends OnTypeBehavior
total_positivetotalconstraintThrows if negative
qty_positiveqtyconstraintThrows if ≤ 0
system_fields_checkcreated_at, modified_atsystemAuto-fill if missing
unique_identifiernameconstraintThrows if duplicate in DB
status_validstatusconstraintThrows if invalid status value
linked_fields_presentcustomer_id, order_idconstraintThrows if missing foreign key

  • Each is non-writable

  • Each has depends_on → runs after the fields they check

  • All use the same resolver as computed/system fields

✅ Takeaways

  1. Guardian schema is just another layer of schema

  2. Each guardian field can compute / mutate / enforce

  3. Depends_on ensures correct execution order

  4. Writable = false ensures protection from user input

  5. Audit log captures mutations and enforcement actions

If you want, I can draft a full JSON example combining:

  • user schema

  • computed schema

  • system schema

  • guardian schema

…with state machine resolution, cascading dependencies, and audit log, ready to use in a CW-like runtime.

Do you want me to do that next?


You said:
lets fist look into some system fields, do guardian touch those on not (as system schema applyed AFTER doctype schema

ChatGPT said:

Perfect — let’s look carefully at system fields vs guardian fields and their interaction.

You said:

system schema is applied after doctype (user/computed) schema

That is key — it determines whether the guardian touches system fields or not.

1️⃣ Field types recap

Field typeSourceWritableWhen resolvedGuardian relevance
User fieldinputyesdoctype schemachecked for constraints
Computed fieldcompute functionnodoctype schemaconstraints may depend on it
System fieldsystem schemano (system-controlled)system schema (after doctype)guardian may inspect but usually not overwrite
Guardian fieldvalidation / enforcementnoguardian schemaenforces rules

2️⃣ Guardian touching system fields

Two options:

A. Guardian only reads system fields

  • Most common pattern

  • System fields are already computed/initialized

  • Guardian checks constraints that depend on system fields (e.g., timestamps, auto IDs)

  • Guardian does not mutate system fields, except maybe filling defaults if explicitly allowed

js
{
  system_fields_check: {
    depends_on: ["created_at", "modified_at"],
    compute: (doc) => {
      if (!doc.created_at) throw new Error("created_at missing"); // read-only enforcement
      if (!doc.modified_at) throw new Error("modified_at missing");
    }
  }
}

✅ This is the safest, predictable approach.

B. Guardian mutates system fields

  • Sometimes guardian acts like a “final fixer”

  • Example: if created_at is missing, guardian can auto-set it

  • Must be explicit and limited to safe fields

js
compute: (doc) => {
  if (!doc.created_at) doc.created_at = new Date(); // system mutation
}

  • Use audit log to track these mutations: cause: 'guardian'

  • Only do this for system fields that are safe to fix

3️⃣ Recommended practice

  1. Do not overwrite user or computed fields in guardian — those should already be resolved.

  2. System fields: guardian can inspect and optionally fix missing or inconsistent system fields

  3. Audit: any mutation by guardian on system fields must be logged.

Example: Full flow

text
1. input fields: qty = 2, price = 5
2. Doctype schema resolves:
    total = price * qty
3. System schema resolves:
    created_at = new Date()
    modified_at = new Date()
4. Guardian schema checks:
    - total > 0
    - qty > 0
    - created_at and modified_at exist (can throw or auto-set)

  • Guardian only reads or safely mutates system fields.

  • User/computed fields are not touched.

✅ Key insight

Guardian is last-stage enforcement:

  • Usually read-only for system fields

  • Can mutate only if necessary (default fill, system repair)

  • Never overwrites user input or computed values

This preserves:

  • Auditability

  • Predictable cascades

  • Separation of concerns (doctype → system → guardian)

If you want, I can make a diagram showing all four layers (user/schema, computed, system, guardian) with arrows for:

  • resolution order

  • which fields guardian can mutate vs only read

  • audit points

This helps visualize exactly how guardian interacts with system fields.

Do you want me to do that?


You said:
i dont want to scatter the responsibilities

ChatGPT said:

Exactly — that’s the core principle here: you don’t want responsibilities scattered across multiple places, because that quickly becomes hard to reason about.

In your layered schema model, you can centralize all responsibilities cleanly while still keeping the system predictable:

1️⃣ Layered responsibility, centralized

Think of your pipeline as four layers, applied sequentially:

LayerResponsibilityMutation scope
Doctype (user + computed)Resolve user input, compute dependent fieldsUser fields + computed fields
System schemaInitialize or recompute system fieldsOnly system fields
Guardian schemaEnforce constraints, validate integrityRead-only: inspect all; Mutate: only safe system defaults if missing
Audit layerTrack all changesLogs all mutations with cause & dependencies

Key points:

  • Each layer has a single clear responsibility

  • State machine handles field-level resolution within each layer

  • No layer mutates fields it shouldn’t:

    • Guardian: rarely mutates, mostly validates

    • System schema: only system fields

    • Doctype schema: only user + computed fields

2️⃣ How to implement without scattering

  1. Keep schemas separate → each has its own depends_on graph

  2. Use the same resolver for all layers → ensures consistent cascades and state tracking

  3. Explicitly declare writable vs read-only fields → prevents accidental overwrites

  4. Audit every mutation → you always know which layer caused the change

  5. No ad hoc checks in compute functions → the resolver handles “should I compute or not?” logic

3️⃣ Example flow

text
Input: { qty: 2, price: 5 }

Step 1: Doctype schema
  total = price * qty
  -> audit: total computed

Step 2: System schema
  created_at = new Date()
  modified_at = new Date()
  -> audit: created_at, modified_at

Step 3: Guardian schema
  - check total > 0
  - check qty > 0
  - check system fields present (can throw or fill defaults)
  -> audit: any fix or violation

Step 4: Audit log contains all mutations

  • Each layer only touches its own fields or read-only validations

  • The state machine handles cascading dependencies inside each schema layer

  • No “hidden logic” in compute functions

4️⃣ Benefits

  • Predictable: every field is resolved exactly once per layer

  • Auditable: all mutations are logged with cause and depends_on

  • Extensible: you can add new system fields or constraints by adding to schema, not scattering code

  • Safe: read-only user/computed fields never overwritten

✅ Takeaway:

  • Keep layered schema approach.

  • Let resolver + state machine handle field dependencies.

  • Guardian is just a last-stage enforcement schema — mostly read-only, optionally fixing only missing system fields.

  • All mutation logic goes through the resolver, audit logs everything, no responsibilities scattered.

If you want, I can draft a concrete JSON-schema example showing user fields, computed fields, system fields, guardian fields, all with dependencies and audit logging, fully integrated — essentially a blueprint for your CW-style runtime.

Do you want me to do that next?


You said:
then basically the whole flow is just managing the inputs() and working with 3 schemas (target_doctype), systemschema and guardian schema

ChatGPT said:

Exactly — that’s the clean way to think about it. 😄

Your entire flow can be reduced to managing input() and sequentially applying three schemas:

1️⃣ The three schemas

SchemaResponsibilityMutation scope
Target / Doctype SchemaResolve user input + computed fieldsUser fields + computed fields
System SchemaInitialize / compute system-only fieldsSystem fields
Guardian SchemaEnforce constraints & integrity rulesMostly read-only: inspect all fields, optionally fix safe system fields

2️⃣ The sequential flow

text
input() → Target / Doctype Schema → System Schema → Guardian Schema → final doc + audit log

Step-by-step

  1. input()

    • User provides values for writable fields

    • Validation ensures read-only fields are protected

  2. Target / Doctype Schema

    • Resolve all computed fields

    • Handle cascading dependencies

    • State machine ensures each field is computed once

    • Audit logs all mutations (user input + computed fields)

  3. System Schema

    • Resolve system fields

    • Can use same resolver / state machine

    • State machine ensures dependencies among system fields are respected

    • Audit logs all system field mutations

  4. Guardian Schema

    • Validate constraints (e.g., totals > 0, qty > 0)

    • Optionally auto-fix missing system fields

    • Audit logs any enforcement or mutation

    • Can throw errors if constraints are violated

3️⃣ Key principles

  1. Single point of mutation: resolver always writes directly to doc

  2. State machine per field: idle → resolving → resolved → error

  3. Audit everything: track field, old/new value, cause (user_input, dependency, system_compute, guardian)

  4. No scattered logic: compute functions are pure — they just read dependencies and return a value, no hidden filtering

  5. Sequential schema application: order enforces responsibilities cleanly

✅ Benefits

  • Predictable, uniform system

  • Easy to reason about cascading dependencies

  • Guardian logic doesn’t interfere with other layers

  • Fully auditable — you know why each field changed

  • Easy to extend: add new computed/system/guardian fields without rewriting the engine

If you want, I can draw a full diagram of this flow, showing:

  • Input → Doctype → System → Guardian

  • Field state machines

  • Cascading dependencies

  • Where mutations and audit logs happen

It would be a very clear blueprint for your runtime.

Do you want me to do that?


You said:
how contraints are defined in Frappe

ChatGPT said:

Perfect — let’s look at how constraints are defined in Frappe and how that maps to your layered schema approach.

1️⃣ Frappe constraint mechanisms

In Frappe/ERPNext, constraints are not “fields” in the traditional sense. They are hooks or rules applied to the document (DocType) before save. They mainly fall into:

Constraint typeHow definedWhen executed
Field-level validationoptions, reqd, unique, data type in DocTypeAutomatic, before save
DocType-level validationvalidate method on DocType classBefore save / submit
Before save hooksbefore_save, before_submitCustom Python functions
On update / submission hookson_update, on_submitAfter save, for cascading or side-effects
SQL / DB constraintsUnique index, foreign keyEnforced at DB level

Example: field-level constraints

python
# DocType field definition
fields = [
    {"fieldname": "qty", "fieldtype": "Int", "reqd": 1},  # cannot be empty
    {"fieldname": "total", "fieldtype": "Currency", "depends_on": "eval:qty*price"}
]

  • reqd → required

  • unique → unique in table

  • depends_on → triggers computation (evaluated dynamically)

Example: DocType-level validation

python
class SalesInvoice(Document):
    def validate(self):
        if self.total < 0:
            frappe.throw("Total cannot be negative")
        if self.qty <= 0:
            frappe.throw("Quantity must be greater than zero")

  • validate() acts as guardian logic: checks fields after user input + computed fields

  • Can throw errors if constraints are violated

Example: hooks on submit

python
def before_submit(doc, method):
    if doc.status != "Paid" and doc.total > 10000:
        frappe.throw("High-value invoices must be approved first")

  • Custom constraint applied conditionally

  • Runs after computed fields but before final save

2️⃣ Mapping to your layered schema approach

Frappe conceptYour CW runtime
Field reqd, unique, optionsPart of doctype schema validation (input validation)
depends_on formula fieldsComputed fields in doctype schema
validate() methodGuardian schema (last-stage enforcement)
before_submit / on_submit hooksOptional post-processing guardian rules or triggers
System fields (creation, modified)System schema

Key insights

  1. Constraints run after computation → fits your guardian schema layer

  2. Field-level constraints can be enforced before resolver → input validation

  3. Guardian schema is exactly like validate() in Frappe:

    • Runs after all doctype + system fields

    • Can inspect all fields

    • Can mutate safe system fields or throw errors

  4. Hooks (before_submit) → optional, can be extra guardian rules triggered by workflow events

3️⃣ How you could define Frappe-style constraints in JSON

json
{
  "guardian_schema": {
    "total_positive": {
      "depends_on": ["total"],
      "compute": "function(doc) { if(doc.total < 0) throw new Error('Total cannot be negative'); }",
      "writable": false
    },
    "qty_positive": {
      "depends_on": ["qty"],
      "compute": "function(doc) { if(doc.qty <= 0) throw new Error('Quantity must be > 0'); }",
      "writable": false
    },
    "high_value_approval": {
      "depends_on": ["status", "total"],
      "compute": "function(doc) { if(doc.total > 10000 && doc.status != 'Paid') throw new Error('High-value invoices must be approved'); }",
      "writable": false
    }
  }
}

  • Each guardian field acts like a constraint rule

  • depends_on ensures the fields are already resolved

  • compute throws or enforces rules

✅ Takeaways

  • Frappe constraints are either field-level, document-level, or hook-based

  • In your system, guardian schema layer mimics Frappe’s validate() and before_submit

  • Input validation + computed fields + system schema + guardian schema = fully layered, predictable enforcement

If you want, I can draw a diagram showing how Frappe’s validate, system fields, and hooks map to your 3-schema + guardian model, so you can see the direct analogy.

Do you want me to do that?


You said:
if we work in this system with 3 layers of schemas and the trigger for any execution is update.inputs streams, how to u see the controller, IF ANY

ChatGPT said:

Ah — now we’re getting into the overall orchestration layer, i.e., the “controller” concept. Let’s reason carefully in the context of your 3-layer schema + state machine + input streams setup.

1️⃣ Current system recap

You have:

  1. Input stream (update.inputs)

    • User updates come in as { field: value }

    • Triggers computation/resolution

  2. Three schema layers

LayerResponsibility
Doctype schemaresolve user + computed fields
System schemaresolve system-only fields
Guardian schemaenforce constraints, validate integrity

  1. Field-level state machines

    • idle → resolving → resolved → error

    • Handles cascading dependencies

  2. Audit / mutation tracking

    • All changes logged with cause & dependencies

2️⃣ Do you need a controller?

Option A: No separate controller

  • Treat update.inputs as the single trigger.

  • Each input triggers state-machine resolver for all layers sequentially:

text
on update.inputs:
  apply doctype schema
  apply system schema
  apply guardian schema

  • Field dependencies are handled inside the resolver

  • No separate orchestration needed

  • Advantages:

    • Minimal architecture, predictable

    • Easy to log all mutations

    • All responsibilities stay in schema layers

  • Disadvantages:

    • No built-in prioritization if some system fields are slow (e.g., async computations)

Option B: Introduce a lightweight controller

  • Controller orchestrates schema application order + triggers + async handling

  • Its role is schema-level orchestration, not field-level logic:

Responsibilities of controller:

  1. Listen to update.inputs stream

  2. Determine which schemas need execution:

    • Doctype always first

    • System schema if relevant fields changed

    • Guardian schema always last

  3. Optionally batch or throttle inputs for efficiency

  4. Pass resolved doc + audit state from layer to layer

Pseudo-code:

js
async function controller(input_stream) {
  for await (const update of input_stream) {
    const doc = await resolveDoctype(update);
    const docWithSystem = await resolveSystem(doc);
    await resolveGuardian(docWithSystem);
    publish(docWithSystem); // propagate final document
  }
}

  • Field dependencies are still handled at resolver level

  • Controller just manages layer execution order + input propagation

3️⃣ Key observations

  1. Field-level state machine + schema layers already handle all cascading logic

    • So controller doesn’t need to micro-manage fields

  2. Controller is optional, but useful if:

    • You have streaming inputs

    • You want batched or incremental processing

    • You want async system computations (e.g., external APIs)

  3. Controller should not contain business logic — leave that in schemas

4️⃣ Visualizing the flow

text
update.inputs → controller? → [Doctype schema] → [System schema] → [Guardian schema] → final doc + audit
                        │
                        └─ optional: throttling, async orchestration, batching

  • Without controller: update.inputs triggers resolver chain directly

  • With controller: it wraps the trigger but leaves computation to schemas

✅ Recommendation

  • Minimal system: no controller needed — just trigger update.inputs → sequential schema application

  • Larger/async system: add a lightweight controller that orchestrates layers and manages streams

If you want, I can draft a diagram showing a streaming input triggering the three schemas with optional controller, including how the state machines, dependencies, and audit logs flow — basically a full CW-style runtime visualization.

Do you want me to do that?


You said:
i want to keep original depends_on with eval. what else i should add to { fieldname: "FIELD_NAME", fieldtype: "FIELD_TYPE", label: "FIELD_LABEL", reqd: 0|1, unique: 0|1, protected: true, auto_generated: true|false, update_on: "create"|"always", hidden: true|false, depends_on: "eval:EXPR", default: <value>, ignore_user_permissions: true|false, oldfieldname: "", oldfieldtype: "" }

ChatGPT said:

Ah — I see exactly what you’re aiming for: a Frappe-style field definition that works in your layered resolver system with audit, system fields, computed fields, and guardian logic, while keeping depends_on expressions intact.

You already have the base properties:

js
{
  fieldname: "FIELD_NAME",
  fieldtype: "FIELD_TYPE",
  label: "FIELD_LABEL",
  reqd: 0|1,
  unique: 0|1,
  protected: true,
  auto_generated: true|false,
  update_on: "create"|"always",
  hidden: true|false,
  depends_on: "eval:EXPR",
  default: <value>,
