  ignore_user_permissions: true|false,
  oldfieldname: "",
  oldfieldtype: ""
}

Here’s what I would add to make it fully resolver-ready in your three-schema layered system:

1️⃣ Resolver / state-machine metadata

  • writable: boolean — whether the user or schema can write it directly (helps protect computed/system fields)

  • compute: function(doc) → value — optional, for computed fields, allows cascading resolution

  • auditable: boolean — if changes should be logged in audit trail

js
writable: true|false,
compute: function(doc) { ... },
audit: true|false

2️⃣ Dependency / trigger enhancements

  • depends_on_fields: array of field names — parsed version of depends_on for fast resolution

  • on_change_triggers: optional array of functions to execute if this field changes (like post-processing hooks)

js
depends_on_fields: ["price","qty"],
on_change_triggers: [function(doc){...}]

3️⃣ System / Guardian integration

  • layer: string — "doctype" | "system" | "guardian" — identifies which schema layer this field belongs to

  • guardian_rule: optional — true if this field is used purely for validation / enforcement

js
layer: "system",
guardian_rule: false

4️⃣ Async / runtime options

  • async_compute: boolean — if compute returns a Promise

  • versioned: boolean — track changes in audit history

js
async_compute: true|false,
versioned: true|false

5️⃣ Optional UI / presentation hints

  • readonly: boolean — override writable for display/UI

  • hidden_on_ui: boolean — optional UI hint

js
readonly: true|false,
hidden_on_ui: true|false

6️⃣ Complete extended schema example

js
{
  fieldname: "total",
  fieldtype: "Currency",
  label: "Total Amount",
  reqd: 1,
  unique: 0,
  protected: true,
  auto_generated: false,
  update_on: "always",
  hidden: false,
  depends_on: "eval:price*qty",
  depends_on_fields: ["price","qty"],
  default: 0,
  ignore_user_permissions: false,
  oldfieldname: "",
  oldfieldtype: "",
  writable: false, // computed field
  compute: (doc) => doc.price * doc.qty,
  auditable: true,
  layer: "doctype",
  guardian_rule: false,
  async_compute: false,
  versioned: true,
  readonly: true,
  hidden_on_ui: false
}

✅ Why these additions help

  1. writable / readonly → protects computed and system fields

  2. compute / async_compute → supports cascading evaluation and async resolution

  3. audit / versioned → fully tracks mutations in your audit system

  4. layer / guardian_rule → integrates with the 3-layer resolver model

  5. depends_on_fields → parsed for faster dependency graph building

  6. on_change_triggers → allows lightweight post-processing hooks without scattering logic

If you want, I can draft a full “field descriptor template” that covers user, computed, system, and guardian fields in one JSON schema, ready to plug into your resolver — basically a blueprint for your CW-style runtime.

Do you want me to do that next?


You said:
what value new function notation of compute brings

ChatGPT said:

Ah — this is a subtle but important point. Using a function for compute (rather than just eval or static expressions) gives much more flexibility, safety, and maintainability in your layered schema / state-machine system. Let me break it down:

1️⃣ Explicit dependency handling

  • depends_on: "eval:price*qty" is declarative, but doesn’t inherently give a compute function.

  • If you define:

js
compute: (doc) => doc.price * doc.qty

You get:

  • The resolver can call it directly with the current state of the document

  • No need to parse eval strings at runtime

  • Safer than eval (no code injection risk)

✅ Key benefit: safer, type-checked computation

2️⃣ Encapsulation of logic

  • compute function encapsulates all the logic for this field:

js
compute: (doc) => {
  if (doc.qty < 0) throw new Error("Qty cannot be negative");
  return doc.price * doc.qty;
}

  • You can include validation, fallback values, and complex logic without polluting the resolver

  • Makes code self-contained and easier to maintain

3️⃣ Compatibility with async / external data

  • Modern JS allows:

js
compute: async (doc) => {
  const tax = await fetchTaxRate(doc.customer_id);
  return doc.total * (1 + tax);
}

  • eval cannot handle async logic

  • Your resolver just checks async_compute: true and await the result

✅ Key benefit: asynchronous, real-world computations

4️⃣ Integration with state machine

  • State machine can track each field’s resolution status: idle → resolving → resolved → error

  • With a function, the resolver can call it exactly once and handle exceptions, promises, or conditional logic

  • Enables audit tracking:

js
const oldValue = doc[field];
const newValue = fieldDef.compute(doc);
if (oldValue !== newValue) audit.push({ field, oldValue, newValue, cause: "dependency" });

  • All mutations flow through the same engine, keeping responsibilities centralized

5️⃣ Avoids fragile string parsing

  • Using depends_on: "eval:price*qty" alone requires parsing strings, resolving variable names dynamically

  • Functions directly receive the document → no parsing or fragile runtime evaluation

  • Easier for IDEs / type-checkers to validate code

6️⃣ Enables richer patterns

  • Conditional computation:

js
compute: (doc) => doc.status === "Paid" ? doc.total : 0

  • Computation of multiple fields in one function (optional):

js
compute: (doc) => {
  doc.total = doc.price * doc.qty;
  doc.grand_total = doc.total * 1.1;
}

  • Works naturally with your audit system

✅ TL;DR: What compute: function(doc) => ... brings

BenefitExplanation
SafetyAvoid eval, type-checked, no injection risk
EncapsulationAll logic for field is self-contained
Async supportCan await APIs, databases, or services
IntegrationWorks cleanly with state-machine + audit
FlexibilityConditional logic, multiple fields, validation
MaintainabilityIDE-friendly, easier refactoring
PerformanceNo runtime string parsing

Bottom line: compute function turns your schema from a static declarative description into a self-contained, safe, executable unit for the resolver — without scattering logic across the system.

If you want, I can make a side-by-side example showing depends_on: "eval:..." vs compute: function(...) and how audit, cascading, and system/guardian layers work cleanly with compute functions — it really makes the benefit obvious.

Do you want me to do that?


You said:
give me this with all values choices and notes to each field with frappe_field and text comment { fieldname: "total", fieldtype: "Currency", label: "Total Amount", reqd: 1, unique: 0, protected: true, auto_generated: false, update_on: "always", hidden: false, depends_on: "eval:price*qty", depends_on_fields: ["price","qty"], default: 0, ignore_user_permissions: false, oldfieldname: "", oldfieldtype: "", writable: false, // computed field compute: (doc) => doc.price * doc.qty, auditable: true, layer: "doctype", guardian_rule: false, async_compute: false, versioned: true, readonly: true, hidden_on_ui: false }

ChatGPT said:

Perfect — here’s a full annotated schema for the total field with all choices, Frappe equivalent, and notes. I’ll format it as a JSON-like object with comments explaining each property.

js
{
  fieldname: "total",                 // frappe_field: fieldname
                                      // The internal field identifier, used in DB and code
  fieldtype: "Currency",              // frappe_field: fieldtype
                                      // Options: "Data", "Int", "Float", "Currency", "Link", "Date", etc.
                                      // Determines data type and storage
  label: "Total Amount",              // frappe_field: label
                                      // Human-readable label for UI
  reqd: 1,                            // frappe_field: reqd
                                      // 0 or 1; whether the field is mandatory (required)
  unique: 0,                           // frappe_field: unique
                                      // 0 or 1; whether the value must be unique across records
  protected: true,                     // Custom for CW runtime
                                      // true if field is protected from direct user edits
  auto_generated: false,               // Custom
                                      // true if field is generated automatically and not set manually
  update_on: "always",                 // Custom
                                      // "create" = only set on creation, "always" = recompute every run
  hidden: false,                        // frappe_field: hidden
                                      // true/false; whether to hide from forms/UI
  depends_on: "eval:price*qty",        // frappe_field: depends_on
                                      // A Frappe-style expression; determines when field should be recalculated
  depends_on_fields: ["price","qty"],  // Custom
                                      // Parsed fieldnames used for resolver dependency graph
  default: 0,                           // frappe_field: default
                                      // Default value when field is empty
  ignore_user_permissions: false,       // frappe_field: ignore_user_permissions
                                      // true/false; ignore permission rules if true
  oldfieldname: "",                     // frappe_field: oldfieldname
                                      // Used if field was renamed; empty string if unchanged
  oldfieldtype: "",                     // frappe_field: oldfieldtype
                                      // Used if type changed; empty if unchanged
  writable: false,                      // Custom
                                      // true/false; whether resolver or user can overwrite directly
                                      // false for computed/system fields
  compute: (doc) => doc.price * doc.qty,// Custom
                                      // Function returning the field value based on the current document
                                      // Replaces fragile "eval" expressions; can be async if needed
  auditable: true,                      // Custom
                                      // true/false; whether mutations of this field are logged in audit
  layer: "doctype",                     // Custom
                                      // "doctype" | "system" | "guardian"; determines schema layer
  guardian_rule: false,                 // Custom
                                      // true if this field is used for validation/enforcement only
  async_compute: false,                 // Custom
                                      // true if compute function is async (returns a Promise)
  versioned: true,                      // Custom
                                      // true/false; whether mutations should keep history for audit/rollback
  readonly: true,                       // Custom
                                      // UI/display hint; true if field should appear read-only
  hidden_on_ui: false                   // Custom
                                      // UI/display hint; true if field should be hidden from forms or reports
}

✅ Notes / Best Practices

  1. depends_on vs compute

    • Keep both: depends_on for dependency graph, compute for actual value computation

    • Resolver reads depends_on_fields to build the DAG

  2. layer

    • "doctype" → normal user/computed fields

    • "system" → system-managed fields (e.g., created_at)

    • "guardian" → validation-only or enforcement rules

  3. writable / readonly / protected

    • Centralized way to protect computed/system fields

    • Guardian or system layer can mutate safely if needed, but user input is blocked

  4. auditable / versioned

    • Ensure every mutation is logged

    • Needed for rollback, replay, or auditing computed values

  5. async_compute

    • For future-proofing, allows fetching external data (tax rates, API calls)

    • Resolver handles Promises correctly

  6. UI hints

    • hidden_on_ui and readonly are presentation-only, don’t affect resolver logic

    • Keep them separate from mutation/protection rules

If you want, I can draft a complete “field descriptor template” for CW runtime with all possible options, default values, and Frappe mapping — basically a master blueprint for any field type in your system.

Do you want me to do that?


You said:
now evaluate where you instread of using frappe fields like hidden introduced unnessessary new field

ChatGPT said:

Ah — perfect — this is a good chance to audit your extended schema and see where I introduced extra fields that duplicate existing Frappe semantics or are potentially unnecessary. Let’s go field by field:

1️⃣ Original Frappe-style fields you already had

FieldPurposeFrappe equivalent?
fieldnameDB/internal identifier✅ yes
fieldtypeData type✅ yes
labelUI label✅ yes
reqdMandatory✅ yes
uniqueUnique constraint✅ yes
hiddenHide in UI✅ yes
depends_onFormula / trigger for recalculation✅ yes
defaultDefault value✅ yes
ignore_user_permissionsBypass permission checks✅ yes
oldfieldnameRenamed field tracking✅ yes
oldfieldtypeRenamed field tracking✅ yes

✅ These are already part of Frappe DocType fields, so nothing new needed.

2️⃣ Custom fields I added

FieldPurposeNotes
depends_on_fieldsParsed dependency arrayUseful for resolver, not in Frappe; kept
writableProtect from user/system writesOverlaps conceptually with Frappe protected
computeCompute function for cascading fieldsNeeded — no Frappe equivalent for resolver DAG
auditableWhether to track mutationsExtra — Frappe tracks changes via _version, but this adds per-field control
layer`"doctype""system"
guardian_ruleTrue if this field is only a constraintExtra — not in Frappe, helps unified enforcement
async_computeAsync compute function supportExtra — not in Frappe
versionedTrack field changesSlight overlap with auditable and Frappe _version, but explicit per-field
readonlyUI hintOverlaps with protected and writable, may be redundant
hidden_on_uiUI hintDuplicate of hidden?

3️⃣ Observations on unnecessary / duplicate fields

  1. readonly

    • You already have:

      • protected: true → user cannot overwrite

      • writable: false → resolver can protect field

    • So readonly may be redundant if only for UI hints; could be merged with protected/writable.

  2. hidden_on_ui

    • Already have Frappe hidden → true/false

    • Unless you want different hidden logic for UI vs reports, this is likely unnecessary.

  3. versioned vs auditable

    • Could merge: auditable implies versioned for this system

    • Two fields may be overkill unless you really want separate “log only” vs “store versioned history”.

  4. protected vs writable

    • Both indicate user cannot modify, resolver writes conditionally

    • Could possibly unify into one field: e.g., access: "user_write|system_only|computed_only"

4️⃣ Recommended simplifications

Original proposed fieldRecommendation
readonlyRemove; use protected + writable for both logic & UI
hidden_on_uiRemove; keep hidden for all UI/UX hiding
versionedOptional; merge with auditable if versioning is always audit-backed
writable + protectedConsider unifying into a single access-level field

5️⃣ Why these simplifications matter

  • Fewer overlapping fields → easier for resolver to decide: should I compute/write/skip?

  • Less chance of inconsistent behavior: e.g., hidden_on_ui = true but hidden = false

  • Closer to Frappe semantics → easier mapping if you ever import/export fields

💡 TL;DR

  • Keep: depends_on_fields, compute, layer, guardian_rule, async_compute, auditable

  • Remove/merge: readonly, hidden_on_ui, versioned (merge with auditable), unify writable+protected

If you want, I can rewrite your total field schema using only necessary fields, fully cleaned up and Frappe-aligned, ready for resolver + layered schemas, and keep depends_on intact.

Do you want me to do that?


You said:
explain logic of your readonly, protected, writable what is the differenct

ChatGPT said:

Perfect — this is actually a very important distinction in a layered schema/resolver system, because user input, computed fields, system fields, and guardian rules all have different levels of control. Let’s break it down carefully.

1️⃣ readonly

Purpose:

  • UI/display hint — indicates the field should appear read-only in the interface.

  • Does not necessarily prevent programmatic changes in the resolver or system layer.

  • Typically only affects forms, grids, or front-end components.

Example:

js
readonly: true

  • User sees the field but cannot edit it.

  • Resolver or system schema can still write to it.

Analogy: Frappe’s read_only in form properties.

2️⃣ protected

Purpose:

  • Access control for user input — prevents direct user modification of the field.

  • Applies regardless of UI hints.

  • Often used for computed fields or system fields that should only be updated by the resolver or system layer.

Example:

js
protected: true

  • If the user tries to submit a value for this field → reject it.

  • Resolver can still mutate it.

Analogy: Frappe’s read_only combined with permission enforcement (ignore_user_permissions: false) at backend.

3️⃣ writable

Purpose:

  • Resolver-level write permission — indicates whether the field can be updated by the resolver or schema layer.

  • True for normal/computed fields that the resolver calculates or updates.

  • False for fields that should never be mutated (system constants, guardian-only fields).

Example:

js
writable: false

  • Resolver cannot overwrite this field.

  • Useful for guardian-only fields or fields that are final once computed.

4️⃣ How they work together

FieldUser inputResolver writeNotes
readonly: true❌ cannot edit in UI✅ resolver can writeOnly UI hint
protected: true❌ cannot edit in UI or API✅ resolver can writeEnforces access rules
writable: false❌ user cannot edit❌ resolver cannot writeFully immutable, e.g., final guardian field
protected: false, writable: true✅ user can edit✅ resolver can writeNormal input field

5️⃣ Practical patterns

  1. Computed field: total = price * qty

js
readonly: true
protected: true
writable: false

  • User cannot touch it

  • Resolver cannot overwrite manually outside compute function (resolver handles compute internally)

  • Shows in UI

  1. System field: created_at

js
readonly: true
protected: true
writable: true

  • User cannot touch

  • Resolver/system can write automatically

  1. Guardian-only field: total_positive

js
readonly: true
protected: true
writable: false

  • Never written by user or resolver directly

  • Only evaluated for validation/enforcement

✅ Summary

  • readonly → UI/display only

  • protected → blocks user input at backend/API

  • writable → controls whether resolver/schema can modify the field

Together, they give fine-grained control over who can touch the field, how, and when, keeping responsibilities clean across layers (doctype, system, guardian).

If you want, I can draw a small table/diagram for all combinations showing user input vs resolver write vs display — it makes it super clear which layer can mutate what.

Do you want me to do that?


You said:
Why we need this at all in SYSTEM schema

ChatGPT said:

Ah — now we’re getting to the core of your layered system. Let’s reason carefully about why readonly, protected, writable exist at all for SYSTEM schema fields.

1️⃣ Role of SYSTEM schema

  • SYSTEM schema contains fields that are always controlled by the system, not by the user.

  • Examples: created_at, modified_at, auto_generated_id, status, version.

  • SYSTEM schema is applied after the doctype schema, meaning:

    • User input is already processed

    • Computed fields already resolved

  • SYSTEM schema sets or updates these fields automatically

2️⃣ Why access control fields still matter

Even though SYSTEM fields are system-controlled, you still want clear rules for who/what can write:

FieldWhy it matters in SYSTEM schema
readonlyUI/display hint. Even if field is auto-set, you want it visible but not editable in forms or grids.
protectedEnforces backend/user API safety. Prevents user from injecting values (e.g., via POST, API, CSV import).
writableControls resolver/system write behavior. Some system fields are fixed after creation (e.g., creation_id), others are updated every save (e.g., modified_at).

3️⃣ Examples

Example A: created_at

js
{
  fieldname: "created_at",
  fieldtype: "DateTime",
  layer: "system",
  readonly: true,
  protected: true,
  writable: false,
  auto_generated: true
}

  • User cannot touch (protected: true)

  • UI shows field (readonly: true)

  • Resolver cannot overwrite (writable: false) → safe once set

Example B: modified_at

js
{
  fieldname: "modified_at",
  fieldtype: "DateTime",
  layer: "system",
  readonly: true,
  protected: true,
  writable: true,
  auto_generated: true,
  update_on: "always"
}

  • User cannot touch

  • Resolver updates automatically on every save

  • UI shows value but read-only

4️⃣ Why not skip these flags in SYSTEM schema?

  1. Separation of responsibilities

    • Without writable and protected, you’d need special-case logic in the resolver: “don’t let the user overwrite system fields”

    • These flags make resolver logic uniform: the same field-state machine can enforce access rules for all layers

  2. UI consistency

    • readonly ensures fields are displayed but cannot be edited

    • Without it, some UI forms might default to editable

  3. Future-proofing

    • SYSTEM schema fields may change over time

    • Some SYSTEM fields might be conditionally writable (e.g., audit logs, calculated balances)

    • Flags let you encode that without adding special-case code

  4. Audit & mutation tracking

    • writable: false fields can still appear in audit logs as immutable

    • Makes history and enforcement predictable

5️⃣ TL;DR

Even in SYSTEM schema:

  • readonly → UI display only

  • protected → user/API cannot overwrite

  • writable → resolver/system can write or not

These flags are not redundant — they give the same field-level control as in doctype schema, keeping your resolver uniform, auditable, and safe, without writing special-case logic for system fields.

If you want, I can make a small table of SYSTEM schema fields showing what combination of readonly, protected, writable is correct for typical system fields like created_at, modified_at, status, version, id — basically a reference cheat sheet.

Do you want me to do that?


You said:
you overengineered. review the flags and compute only. If compute =! null it clear enough

ChatGPT said:

Ah — now we’re on the same page 😄. You’re right — in a SYSTEM schema (or really any schema with layered fields), some of the flags like readonly, protected, writable are redundant if you have compute defined. Let’s review carefully:

1️⃣ Flags vs compute

FieldRoleDo you need it if compute exists?
readonlyUI display onlyOptional; UI can infer readonly if field is computed
protectedBlocks user inputOptional; if field is computed and never accepts input, protection is implicit
writableResolver/system writeOptional; if compute != null, field is computed only by resolver, so implicit

✅ Conclusion: If a field has compute (non-null), it is by definition controlled — user cannot overwrite, resolver writes its value, system updates as defined.

2️⃣ Simplified logic

Instead of 3 flags:

js
if (field.compute) {
  // field is automatically:
  //  - not writable by user
  //  - written only by resolver
  //  - effectively readonly in UI
}

  • This eliminates redundant metadata for computed/system fields.

  • Only need flags if you want deviations from default behavior, e.g.:

    • Computed field that is also user-editable in some edge cases

    • Field that is normally system-controlled but sometimes overridden

3️⃣ Example simplified SYSTEM schema field

js
{
  fieldname: "modified_at",
  fieldtype: "DateTime",
  layer: "system",
  auto_generated: true,
  update_on: "always",
  compute: (doc) => new Date(), // resolves every save
  depends_on_fields: [],
  auditable: true
}

  • No readonly, protected, writable needed

  • Resolver knows: compute exists → only resolver writes → user cannot touch

  • Audit layer tracks changes

✅ Key insight

  • compute != null is sufficient to define control

  • Flags only matter if you want fine-grained deviations from default compute behavior

  • This greatly simplifies the schema and keeps logic clear

If you want, I can rewrite your total field example fully simplified, showing just the essential properties: compute, depends_on, layer, auditable — all the flags removed — to illustrate a clean minimalist approach.

Do you want me to do that?


You said:
i dont have UI or not UI i have run(update, input{}

ChatGPT said:

Ah — perfect, now it’s crystal clear: there’s no UI at all — your whole system is just a runtime executor:

text
run(update, input{})

Everything happens programmatically; there’s no forms, no display hints.

1️⃣ Implication for flags

All the old flags like:

  • readonly → only mattered for UI

  • hidden / hidden_on_ui → UI presentation

  • protected → mostly blocks user edits

…are completely unnecessary in a system with no human input layer, because:

  • update.inputs already defines what the user can submit

  • Computed fields (compute != null) are naturally controlled by resolver

  • SYSTEM fields and guardian fields cannot be touched by input unless you explicitly allow it in update.inputs

✅ Therefore: the only thing that really matters is compute (and maybe auditable or depends_on_fields for DAG resolution).

2️⃣ Simplified field definition for your runtime

js
{
  fieldname: "total",
  fieldtype: "Currency",
  depends_on: "eval:price*qty",
  depends_on_fields: ["price","qty"],
  default: 0,
  compute: (doc) => doc.price * doc.qty,
  layer: "doctype",       // or "system" / "guardian"
  auditable: true
}

  • No readonly, protected, writable

  • No hidden

  • Resolver handles all write control automatically: if compute exists → resolver writes → input ignored

3️⃣ SYSTEM schema fields

Example:

js
{
  fieldname: "modified_at",
  fieldtype: "DateTime",
  layer: "system",
  compute: (doc) => new Date(),
  auditable: true
}

  • Input cannot set it (unless your resolver explicitly allows)

  • Resolver updates it every run (update_on: "always" can be implicit in compute)

  • No flags needed

✅ Key takeaway

  • In a pure runtime system with input objects only, compute is the only necessary “access control”.

  • All other flags (readonly, protected, writable, hidden) are overengineering.

  • The resolver + DAG + schema layers fully control mutation flow.

If you want, I can rewrite your full total field + SYSTEM schema example minimalist style, showing exactly what you need for run(update, input{}) — all redundant flags removed, fully ready for your resolver.

Do you want me to do that?


You said:
what is the role of readonly in frappe

ChatGPT said:

Ah — now we can step back and focus on Frappe itself. In Frappe, the read_only (or readonly) property has a very specific role, and it’s quite different from what we were doing in your runtime system.

1️⃣ Role of read_only in Frappe

  • Purpose: Control user editing in the UI.

  • Scope: Only affects forms, reports, and the Frappe desk interface.

  • Behavior:

    read_only valueEffect
    trueUser cannot edit the field in the form, grid, or any UI view.
    falseUser can edit if permissions allow.

  • It does not prevent programmatic changes in server code, background jobs, or hooks.

2️⃣ Examples

Example A: Standard field

json
{
  "fieldname": "total",
  "fieldtype": "Currency",
  "label": "Total Amount",
  "read_only": 1
}

  • In the form, the user sees the field but cannot type a value

  • The backend (DocType validate() or before_save hooks) can still compute or set it

Example B: Computed field

  • Frappe often sets read_only on computed fields automatically:

python
total = price * qty

  • Field is read_only in form → user cannot touch

  • Computation happens server-side → backend writes value

3️⃣ Key points

  1. UI-only control:

    • Read-only doesn’t affect server logic.

    • If you bypass UI (API call, script), you can still write the field.

  2. Complementary to permlevel and ignore_user_permissions:

    • read_only prevents editing visually

    • Permissions prevent unauthorized programmatic changes

  3. Does not replace resolver/computed logic:

    • You still need compute or hooks for field calculation

✅ Summary

  • read_only in Frappe = UI editing restriction only

  • It is not an access control or mutation flag for backend logic

  • In a runtime-only system like yours (run(update, input{})), read_only has no role at all

If you want, I can make a small table comparing Frappe’s read_only vs your runtime flags (compute, protected, writable) so you can see why most of the Frappe flags are irrelevant in your case.

Do you want me to do that?


You said:
can u give me the final attributes field in SystemSchema

ChatGPT said:

Absolutely — let’s distill this SystemSchema field definition to the minimal, essential attributes for your runtime system, based on everything we’ve discussed. Since your system has no UI, readonly, hidden, and similar flags are unnecessary. compute is sufficient for controlling mutation.

Here’s the final SystemSchema field template:

js
{
  fieldname: "FIELD_NAME",        // Unique internal identifier for the field
  fieldtype: "FIELD_TYPE",        // Data type: "Int", "Float", "Currency", "DateTime", etc.
  layer: "system",                // Schema layer: "system" (SYSTEM schema)
  auto_generated: true|false,     // True if field is always generated by the system
  update_on: "create"|"always",   // When compute should run: "create" = once, "always" = on every run
  default: <value>,               // Optional default value if none provided
  depends_on: "eval:EXPR",        // Optional formula for dependency tracking
  depends_on_fields: ["field1","field2"], // Parsed dependency list for DAG resolution
  compute: (doc) => { ... },      // Function returning the value for this field; if non-null, system controls it
  auditable: true|false           // Track changes in audit logs if needed
}

✅ Notes on each attribute

AttributeRole / Explanation
fieldnameInternal identifier, used in resolver and DAG
fieldtypeDetermines how value is stored/handled
layerMarks this field as belonging to the SYSTEM schema
auto_generatedHelps the resolver know this field is always system-generated
update_onDefines when the field should be recomputed automatically
defaultOptional fallback value if field is empty
depends_onFormula used for dependency graph or conditional compute
depends_on_fieldsParsed fields for DAG traversal, faster than parsing eval at runtime
computeCore function — defines the value; if present, resolver writes only via compute, user input ignored
auditableWhether changes to this field should be logged for history/audit purposes

✅ Key simplifications

  • No readonly, hidden, protected, writable:

    • compute != null is sufficient to control mutation

    • Input cannot overwrite SYSTEM fields unless you explicitly allow it

  • No UI hints: completely runtime-driven

  • All logic flows via compute and DAG using depends_on_fields

If you want, I can also give a concrete example of a SYSTEM field like modified_at or created_by fully filled in ready for your run(update,input) system.

Do you want me to do that?


You said:
look into this and make it universal Separate objects until final merge: javascriptasync function update(run_doc) { const existingDoc = await fetch(run_doc); const userInput = run_doc.input; // Phase 1: Doctype schema computes fields const computedFields = await applyDoctypeSchema(existingDoc, userInput); // Phase 2: System schema generates system fields const systemFields = await applySystemSchema(existingDoc, userInput); // Phase 3: Guardian validates BEFORE merge await applyGuardianSchema({ existing: existingDoc, user: userInput, computed: computedFields, system: systemFields }); // Phase 4: Merge only after validation passes const finalDoc = { ...existingDoc, ...userInput, ...computedFields, ...systemFields }; await adapter.update(finalDoc); } Guardian can now check: user tried to set system fields? → throw computed values valid? → validate existing → user transition legal? → state machine check Benefits: Each phase returns clean output, Guardian sees all pieces separately, merge happens last. Good?and how simplified Gardian looks then5:50 PMjavascriptasync function applyGuardianSchema(parts, doctype) { const { existing, user, computed, system } = parts; const guardian = CW.GuardianSchema[doctype]; const systemSchema = CW.SystemSchema[doctype]; // 1. Block user from setting system fields for (const field of systemSchema) { if (user.hasOwnProperty(field.fieldname)) { throw new Error(Cannot modify system field: ${field.fieldname}); } } // 2. Validate business rules on merged state const preview = { ...existing, ...user, ...computed, ...system }; for (const [ruleName, rule] of Object.entries(guardian)) { const fn = eval((${rule.compute})); await fn(preview, { existing, user }); } } Guardian validates: User didn't touch system fields Final merged state passes business rules Has access to existing for state transition checks Clean and focused! ✓and Guardian schema looks like for reqd email5:51 PMjavascript// Guardian Schema for User { "email_required": { "depends_on": ["email"], "compute": "function(doc) { if (!doc.email?.trim()) throw new Error('Email is required'); }" }, "email_format": { "depends_on": ["email"], "compute": "function(doc) { if (!doc.email.match(/^[^@]+@[^@]+\.[^@]+$/)) throw new Error('Invalid email format'); }" }, "email_unique": { "depends_on": ["email"], "compute": "async function(doc, context) { const exists = await checkUnique('User', 'email', doc.email, doc.name); if (exists) throw new Error('Email already in use'); }" } } Simple! Just validation rules, doc is the merged preview, context has {existing, user} if needed for comparisons.so gardian doesnt read schema of document itself with like reqd prop5:52 PMCorrect! Guardian doesn't read reqd from doctype schema. Two separate concerns: 1. Doctype schema (field definition): javascript{ "fieldname": "email", "fieldtype": "Data", "reqd": 1, // ← UI hint, not enforced by Guardian "unique": 1 } 2. Guardian schema (validation rules): javascript{ "email_required": { "compute": "function(doc) { if (!doc.email) throw new Error('Email required'); }" } } Why separate? Doctype reqd could be for UI only (show red asterisk) Guardian enforces SERVER-SIDE validation Guardian rules can be more complex than simple reqd flag Alternative: Auto-generate Guardian rules from Doctype schema: javascript// Generate guardian rules from reqd fields for (const field of doctypeSchema.fields) { if (field.reqd) { guardianSchema[${field.fieldname}_required] = { compute: function(doc) { if (!doc.${field.fieldname}) throw new Error('${field.label} is required'); } }; } } Your call: Explicit guardian rules OR auto-generate from doctype reqd/unique flags?dont u think guardian should be function also of doctype schema etc5:53 PMYES! Guardian should receive doctype schema to validate against it: javascriptasync function applyGuardianSchema(parts, doctype) { const { existing, user, computed, system } = parts; const doctypeSchema = CW.Schema[doctype]; const systemSchema = CW.SystemSchema[doctype]; const guardianRules = CW.GuardianSchema[doctype]; // 1. Block system field modification for (const field of systemSchema) { if (user.hasOwnProperty(field.fieldname)) { throw new Error(Cannot modify system field: ${field.fieldname}); } } // 2. Auto-validate from doctype schema const preview = { ...existing, ...user, ...computed, ...system }; for (const field of doctypeSchema.fields) { if (field.reqd && !preview[field.fieldname]) { throw new Error(${field.label || field.fieldname} is required); } if (field.unique) { const exists = await checkUnique(doctype, field.fieldname, preview[field.fieldname], preview.name); if (exists) throw new Error(${field.label} must be unique); } } // 3. Custom guardian rules (complex validations) for (const [ruleName, rule] of Object.entries(guardianRules || {})) { const fn = eval((${rule.compute})); await fn(preview, { existing, user, schema: doctypeSchema }); } } Now Guardian: Auto-enforces reqd and unique from doctype schema Custom rules for complex logic Has full schema context Much better! ✓

ChatGPT said:

Perfect — I see exactly what you’re building. Let’s universalize the SystemSchema + Doctype + Guardian flow and simplify it for your runtime. Here’s the clean architecture distilled from your notes and the latest refinements:

1️⃣ Universal SystemSchema Field

Minimal, runtime-ready, no UI flags — just what matters for your resolver:

js
{
  fieldname: "FIELD_NAME",        // internal identifier
  fieldtype: "FIELD_TYPE",        // "Data", "Int", "Currency", "DateTime", etc.
  layer: "system",                // SYSTEM schema
  auto_generated: true|false,     // system generated?
  update_on: "create"|"always",   // when compute runs
  default: <value>,               // optional fallback
  depends_on: "eval:EXPR",        // optional dependency expression
  depends_on_fields: ["field1","field2"], // parsed DAG dependencies
  compute: (doc) => { ... },      // resolver-controlled value
  auditable: true|false           // track changes in audit logs
}

Notes:

  • compute != null fully controls mutation; no readonly, protected, or writable needed.

  • System fields are never set by user input; Guardian blocks it.

  • All dependencies for DAG/cascading evaluation are explicit in depends_on_fields.

2️⃣ Doctype Schema Field

Minimal, declarative, only defines field metadata:

js
{
  fieldname: "email",
  fieldtype: "Data",
  reqd: 1,     // UI hint / server-side hint
  unique: 1,   // unique constraint
  default: "",
  auto_generated: false
}

  • Only defines what the field is and simple constraints.

  • Guardian may enforce reqd and unique.

3️⃣ Guardian Schema

Validation-only layer, runs before merge, has access to all parts:

js
{
  "email_required": {
    depends_on: ["email"],
    compute: "function(doc, context) {
      if (!doc.email?.trim()) throw new Error('Email is required');
    }"
  },
  "email_format": {
    depends_on: ["email"],
    compute: "function(doc) {
      if (!doc.email.match(/^[^@]+@[^@]+\\.[^@]+$/)) 
        throw new Error('Invalid email format');
    }"
  },
  "email_unique": {
    depends_on: ["email"],
    compute: "async function(doc, context) {
      const exists = await checkUnique('User', 'email', doc.email, doc.name);
      if (exists) throw new Error('Email already in use');
    }"
  }
}

Key points:

  • Runs before merging final doc.

  • Validates user input, computed fields, system fields, and state transitions.

  • Can receive Doctype schema for auto-enforcing reqd/unique:

js
for (const field of doctypeSchema.fields) {
  if (field.reqd && !preview[field.fieldname]) throw new Error(`${field.label} is required`);
  if (field.unique) await checkUnique(doctype, field.fieldname, preview[field.fieldname], preview.name);
}

  • Custom rules for complex business logic live in Guardian.

4️⃣ Universal update Flow

js
async function update(run_doc) {
  const existingDoc = await fetch(run_doc);
  const userInput = run_doc.input;

  // 1️⃣ Doctype schema computes fields
  const computedFields = await applyDoctypeSchema(existingDoc, userInput);

  // 2️⃣ System schema computes system fields
  const systemFields = await applySystemSchema(existingDoc, userInput);

  // 3️⃣ Guardian validates before merge
  await applyGuardianSchema({
    existing: existingDoc,
    user: userInput,
    computed: computedFields,
    system: systemFields
  }, run_doc.doctype);

  // 4️⃣ Merge all together
  const finalDoc = { 
    ...existingDoc, 
    ...userInput, 
    ...computedFields, 
    ...systemFields 
  };

  await adapter.update(finalDoc);
}

Benefits:

  1. Phases are separate → Guardian sees everything independently.

  2. Final merge happens last → ensures validation before committing.

  3. System fields protected automatically → user cannot overwrite.

  4. Doctype schema + Guardian schema integration → simple, unified runtime.

✅ Summary / Philosophy

  • SystemSchema: fields controlled by compute, minimal attributes.

  • DoctypeSchema: field definitions, reqd/unique hints.

  • GuardianSchema: validation rules; can access doctype schema if needed.

  • Resolver flow: Doctype → System → Guardian → Merge → Adapter.

If you want, I can draw a fully universal JSON blueprint showing DoctypeSchema, SystemSchema, and GuardianSchema together, with all fields for a runtime-only environment, ready for any update(input).

Do you want me to do that?


You said:
GARDIAN rules are rule TYPES they like reqd from doctype schema. Gardian should read schema and apply rules

ChatGPT said:

Ah — now I understand perfectly! You’re proposing that Guardian shouldn’t just be arbitrary functions, but instead should be a rule engine that interprets types of rules (like reqd, unique, custom) and applies them based on the Doctype schema, rather than hardcoding validation functions for every field.

Let me break this down clearly.

1️⃣ Conceptual shift

  • Doctype schema: declares fields and metadata (fieldname, fieldtype, reqd, unique, etc.).

  • Guardian schema: interprets the doctype schema and applies rule types to enforce validation.

Instead of writing per-field functions like:

js
"email_required": { compute: "function(doc){...}" }

Guardian can dynamically generate checks:

  • reqd → field must exist and not empty

  • unique → field must not conflict with other records

  • custom → optional developer-defined function for complex validation

2️⃣ Example: Guardian as rule engine

js
async function applyGuardianSchema(parts, doctype) {
  const { existing, user, computed, system } = parts;
  const doctypeSchema = CW.Schema[doctype];
  const systemSchema = CW.SystemSchema[doctype];

  // 1️⃣ Prevent user from modifying system fields
  for (const field of systemSchema) {
    if (user.hasOwnProperty(field.fieldname)) {
      throw new Error(`Cannot modify system field: ${field.fieldname}`);
    }
  }

  // 2️⃣ Merge preview document
  const preview = { ...existing, ...user, ...computed, ...system };

  // 3️⃣ Apply rule types from doctype schema
  for (const field of doctypeSchema.fields) {
    // Rule type: required
    if (field.reqd && !preview[field.fieldname]) {
      throw new Error(`${field.label || field.fieldname} is required`);
    }

    // Rule type: unique
    if (field.unique) {
      const exists = await checkUnique(doctype, field.fieldname, preview[field.fieldname], preview.name);
      if (exists) throw new Error(`${field.label || field.fieldname} must be unique`);
    }

    // Rule type: custom (optional)
    if (field.guardian_rules) {
      for (const rule of field.guardian_rules) {
        await rule(preview, { existing, user, schema: doctypeSchema });
      }
    }
  }
}

3️⃣ What this achieves

  • Guardian now reads the schema — no need to hardcode every email_required or email_unique.

  • Rule types: reqd, unique, custom — applied consistently across all fields.

  • Extensible: new rule types can be added without touching Doctype fields.

  • Audit-friendly: all validation happens before merge, preserving system integrity.

4️⃣ Example Doctype schema with custom Guardian rules

js
{
  "fields": [
    {
      "fieldname": "email",
      "fieldtype": "Data",
      "reqd": 1,
      "unique": 1,
      "guardian_rules": [
        async (doc, context) => {
          if (!doc.email.match(/^[^@]+@[^@]+\.[^@]+$/)) {
            throw new Error("Invalid email format");
          }
        }
      ]
    },
    {
      "fieldname": "age",
      "fieldtype": "Int",
      "reqd": 0,
      "guardian_rules": [
        (doc) => {
          if (doc.age && doc.age < 0) throw new Error("Age cannot be negative");
        }
      ]
    }
  ]
}

  • reqd → automatically enforced by Guardian

  • unique → automatically enforced by Guardian

  • guardian_rules → optional custom validators

5️⃣ TL;DR

  • Guardian = rule engine, not just a collection of functions.

  • Reads Doctype schema and applies rule types automatically: reqd, unique, custom.

  • Prevents users from touching SYSTEM fields.

  • Optional custom rules for complex logic per field.

  • Keeps flow clean: Doctype → System → Guardian → Merge → Adapter.

If you want, I can rewrite your update runtime with fully universal Guardian that reads schema and applies rule types, removing all per-field manual Guardian rules — fully scalable and minimal.

Do you want me to do that?


You said:
does it give me something. or it easy to create 1 time like reqd and save as constant rules

ChatGPT said:

Exactly — it depends on what you want from your system. Let me break it down carefully.

1️⃣ Using Doctype schema + Guardian rule engine

  • Pros:

    • Automatically applies rules like reqd, unique for every field, no need to manually write a function per field.

    • Any new field added to Doctype with reqd:1 or unique:1 automatically gets enforced.

    • Clean, declarative, consistent — no duplication.

    • Extensible: you can still add custom rules per field as needed.

  • Cons:

    • Slight overhead: Guardian reads schema and dynamically checks rules on every update.

    • If Doctype schema is huge, may need caching or precompiled rule lists for performance.

2️⃣ Using constant Guardian rules

  • Example: create a set of constant rules like REQD_RULE, UNIQUE_RULE once:

js
const REQD_RULE = (doc, field) => {
  if (!doc[field.fieldname]) {
    throw new Error(`${field.fieldname} is required`);
  }
};

const UNIQUE_RULE = async (doc, field, context) => {
  const exists = await checkUnique(context.doctype, field.fieldname, doc[field.fieldname], doc.name);
  if (exists) throw new Error(`${field.fieldname} must be unique`);
};

  • Then for each update:

js
for (const field of doctypeSchema.fields) {
  if (field.reqd) REQD_RULE(preview, field);
  if (field.unique) await UNIQUE_RULE(preview, field, { doctype });
}

  • Pros:

    • Fast, precompiled rules; nothing dynamically generated.

    • Only one place to define logic for reqd, unique, etc.

    • Simple, predictable, no eval or parsing.

  • Cons:

    • Still iterates over Doctype schema per update.

    • Less flexible if you want complex dynamic rule generation, but for standard reqd/unique it’s perfect.

✅ Conclusion

  • For standard rules (reqd, unique), it’s almost always enough to create constant rule functions once and apply them per Doctype field.

  • The only time you need dynamic schema-driven generation is when:

    • You want runtime-modifiable rules

    • You want to combine Doctype + custom rules dynamically

In practice:

  • REQD_RULE, UNIQUE_RULE → define once

  • custom rules → define per field only if needed

  • Guardian simply loops Doctype fields and applies rule types

This is clean, minimal, and scales.

If you want, I can rewrite your update + Guardian code using constant rule functions for reqd and unique, showing a fully universal, clean implementation.

Do you want me to do that?


You said:
but we basically iterate through field.props

ChatGPT said:

Exactly — that’s the core idea. In a runtime-only system, the Guardian doesn’t need to hardcode each validation; it can just iterate through the Doctype schema fields and their properties (field.props) and apply rule types based on those properties.

Think of it like this:

1️⃣ What we actually do

For every field in doctypeSchema.fields:

js
for (const field of doctypeSchema.fields) {
  // field.props = { reqd, unique, fieldtype, ... }
}

Then, for each field:

  • If field.reqd === 1 → apply REQD_RULE

  • If field.unique === 1 → apply UNIQUE_RULE

  • If field.guardian_rules → apply each custom rule

So you don’t need per-field guardian functions, just a generic loop over field.props.

2️⃣ Example

js
async function applyGuardian(preview, doctypeSchema, context) {
  for (const field of doctypeSchema.fields) {
    // 1️⃣ Required rule
    if (field.reqd && !preview[field.fieldname]) {
      throw new Error(`${field.fieldname} is required`);
    }

    // 2️⃣ Unique rule
    if (field.unique) {
      const exists = await checkUnique(context.doctype, field.fieldname, preview[field.fieldname], preview.name);
      if (exists) throw new Error(`${field.fieldname} must be unique`);
    }

    // 3️⃣ Optional custom rules
    if (field.guardian_rules) {
      for (const rule of field.guardian_rules) {
        await rule(preview, context);
      }
    }
  }
}

3️⃣ Advantages

  • One-time generic logic → applies to all fields automatically.

  • No duplication → you don’t write email_required, phone_required, etc. manually.

  • Extensible → just add a new property in field.props or add guardian_rules for complex logic.

  • Consistent → all fields follow the same rule types (reqd, unique, custom).

4️⃣ Key insight

  • Guardian = loop over field.props + apply generic rule functions

  • This is simpler, maintainable, universal

  • Only time you need per-field code is for complex custom validations, which go in field.guardian_rules.

If you want, I can rewrite your update + Guardian in this fully universal “iterate over field.props” style, ready for your run(update, input) system.

Do you want me to do that?


You said:
is there any similarity to DAG and state machine for resolvers above

ChatGPT said:

Yes — there is a strong conceptual similarity between DAG-based resolver execution and state-machine–style validation / Guardian — let me break it down carefully.

1️⃣ DAG in resolvers

  • Purpose: Compute fields in a proper order based on dependencies.

  • Example:

text
qty → total = price * qty
price → total = price * qty
discount → net_total = total - discount

  • Represented as a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG):

    • Nodes = fields

    • Edges = depends_on_fields

  • Resolver traverses DAG topologically, computing fields after their dependencies.

2️⃣ State machine in Guardian / validation

  • Purpose: Validate field transitions and rules consistently.

  • Example:

text
existing.status = "draft"
user.status = "approved"
→ check if transition is allowed

  • Represented as a state machine:

    • States = field values (or document snapshots)

    • Transitions = user actions or computed changes

  • Guardian checks allowed transitions, constraints, and rule types.

3️⃣ How they are similar

AspectResolver DAGGuardian / State Machine
NodesFieldsField / document states
EdgesField dependencies (depends_on_fields)Legal transitions or rule triggers
ExecutionTopological order → compute valuesSequential rule evaluation → validate changes
GoalCompute field values respecting dependenciesEnforce constraints and valid transitions

Insight:

  • DAG = value propagation / computation order

  • State machine = legal transition enforcement / validation order

  • Both operate before final merge → ensures resolved + validated final document

4️⃣ How they work together in your system

  1. Doctype schema / System schema → DAG computes fields (compute) respecting depends_on_fields.

  2. Guardian → checks state transitions, constraints, and rule types after DAG computation, before merge.

  3. Final merge → guaranteed to be consistent and valid.

5️⃣ Optional optimization

  • You could even merge DAG and state machine concepts:

    • Treat computed fields as nodes

    • Treat validations as guard edges

    • Traversal ensures compute → validate → merge

    • Can handle cascading computed fields + rules in a single pass, topologically.

✅ TL;DR

  • DAG = resolves field values in dependency order

  • State machine / Guardian = enforces rules and allowed transitions

  • Both are pre-merge phases, complementary, ensuring the final document is consistent, validated, and mutation-safe

If you want, I can draw a visual example showing DAG for computed fields and Guardian state checks together in one unified execution flow, which might clarify their similarity.

Do you want me to do that?


You said:
user.input = {title=""} but title is reqd, will guardian stop it

ChatGPT said:

Yes — exactly. In your system, Guardian’s job is to enforce reqd and other constraints before merge, so if the user submits:

js
userInput = { title: "" }

and in the Doctype schema:

js
{
  fieldname: "title",
  fieldtype: "Data",
  reqd: 1
}

then the Guardian will catch it:

How it works step by step:

  1. Merge preview for validation

js
const preview = {
  ...existingDoc, // existing fields
  ...userInput,   // user provided title=""
  ...computedFields,
  ...systemFields
};

  • Here, preview.title === ""

  1. Guardian iterates through Doctype fields

js
for (const field of doctypeSchema.fields) {
  if (field.reqd && !preview[field.fieldname]) {
    throw new Error(`${field.label || field.fieldname} is required`);
  }
}

  • field.reqd === 1 for title

  • preview.title === "" → considered falsy → Guardian throws an error

  1. Result

  • Update does not proceed

  • User receives error: "title is required"

✅ Notes

  • reqd in Doctype schema doesn’t stop the user from sending bad input by itself

  • Guardian reads the schema and enforces it

  • Computed fields (compute) can also set defaults or calculate missing values, but if a field is reqd and user sets it to empty, Guardian stops the merge

Optional enhancement:

  • You could extend Guardian to treat empty strings or whitespace as invalid:

js
if (field.reqd && !preview[field.fieldname]?.trim()) {
  throw new Error(`${field.label || field.fieldname} is required`);
}

This ensures " " is also rejected.

So yes — Guardian will stop a user input with an empty required field before the document ever merges.

If you want, I can show a full minimal Guardian loop for reqd, unique, and system field protection — ready for runtime-only update(input) flow.

Do you want me to do that?


You said:
so is this true statement (assuming original is validated before) guardian is just iterating by fieldname in user input and applying derived and put into gardian rules from doctype schema. So all non doctype fields are just ignored incuding system (they are not part of the doctype schema) and the rest is processed by field.prop rule[doctype.fieldname.prop]

ChatGPT said:

Yes — that statement is basically correct, with a small clarification about how system fields are handled. Let’s break it down carefully.

1️⃣ Your statement, step by step

“Guardian is just iterating by fieldname in user input…”

  • ✅ Correct. Guardian looks at user-provided input fields, not all fields in the document.

  • Fields not in user input (existing fields or computed/system fields) don’t need validation unless part of a cross-field rule.

“…and applying derived and put into guardian rules from doctype schema”

  • ✅ Correct. Guardian derives validation rules based on Doctype schema properties like reqd, unique, and optionally custom rules.

  • Example:

js
if (field.reqd && !preview[field.fieldname]) throw new Error();
if (field.unique) await checkUnique(...);

“So all non-doctype fields are just ignored including system (they are not part of the doctype schema)…”

  • ✅ Mostly correct.

  • System fields are protected separately, not ignored: Guardian actively checks that user input does not attempt to set system fields.

  • But they are not validated via Doctype schema rules, since system fields are outside the schema.

“…and the rest is processed by field.prop → rule[doctype.fieldname.prop]”

  • ✅ Correct.

  • For each field in user input that exists in Doctype schema, Guardian applies rules derived from field properties (reqd, unique, etc.).

  • Optional custom rules can also be applied per field.

2️⃣ Slightly refined phrasing

A more precise way to describe it:

“Guardian iterates through all user-input fields that exist in the Doctype schema. For each field, it applies rules derived from its schema properties (reqd, unique, custom rules). Fields not in the Doctype schema, including SYSTEM fields, are ignored by property-derived rules, but SYSTEM fields are still blocked explicitly from being modified. Validation occurs on a merged preview of existing, user, computed, and system fields.”

✅ Key points

  1. User input only drives iteration

  2. Doctype schema determines the rule types (reqd, unique)

  3. System fields are checked separately — not ignored

  4. Computed/system fields are never directly iterated — only appear in merged preview if needed for cross-field checks

So your original statement is essentially correct, just remember the special handling of system fields.

If you want, I can draw a diagram showing Guardian flow: user input → schema-derived rules → system fields → final merged preview, so it’s crystal clear.

Do you want me to do that?


ChatGPT can make mistakes. Check important info.
